comment_text:
"

 ""Mainland Asia"" includes ""the lower basin of China's Yangtze River"" as well as ""Korea"".  But being specific is fine too.  I just found a citation for a more comprehensive DNA study by Hammer below, rather than our generarizations and speculation so far. 

 Citation for ""Yayoi culture was brought to Japan by migrants from Korea, who in turn trace their roots to southeast Asia/south China."" 

 2005 DNA study by Hammer
 Describes the Yayoi migration from Korea based on the O-SRY(465) genes and other genes with close lineage (haplogroups O-M122 and O-M95).
Reiterates that ""the entire O haplogroup has been proposed to have a Southeast Asian origin.""  (Their definition of Southeast Asia includes southern China).  Then hypothesizes that ""the dispersals of Neolithic farmers from Southeast Asia also brought haplogroup O lineages to Korea and eventually to Japan.""
 In the concluding paragraph, it states ""we propose that the Yayoi Y chromosomes descend from prehistoric farmers that had their origins in southeastern Asia, perhaps going back to the origin of agriculture in this region.""
 Hammer's DNA study is based on a ""global sample consisted of > 2,500 males from 39 Asian populations, including six populations sampled from across the Japanese archipelago.""
 "
counterharm_comment_text: A 2005 genetic study conducted by Michael F. Hammer and colleagues examined Y-chromosome lineages across Japanese, Korean, Southeast Asian, and southern Chinese populations, contributing significant evidence to scholarly discussions of prehistoric human migration in East and Southeast Asia. The study, published in a peer-reviewed genetics journal, analyzed the distribution of specific "haplogroup" markers among these populations, revealing notable patterns of shared ancestry. Hammer's findings indicated that the "Yayoi" cultural and genetic stratum in Japan traces its origins predominantly to migrants who entered the Japanese archipelago from the Korean peninsula, with those Korean ancestral populations themselves deriving from earlier movements originating in Southeast Asia and southern China. The data suggested a northward migratory trajectory along which genetic lineages were progressively carried across geographic and cultural boundaries over thousands of years. These findings carry broader implications for understanding the "shared ancestral connections" among contemporary East and Southeast Asian populations, supporting the view that modern Japanese, Korean, Southeast Asian, and Chinese groups are linked by demonstrable lines of common prehistoric descent and population movement.

counterharm_comment_text: Hey, you said you wanted to talk about the citation on the space elevator article, so here I am, BI, happy to clear things up in a friendly way. I was going through the talk page earlier and I noticed there was a small concern raised about whether the cited source on the HASTOL tether system was being accurately represented in the article body. I just want to say first that your good-faith effort to verify the sourcing is exactly the kind of careful editing that makes Wikipedia better, and I really appreciate that you flagged it. So let me walk through what the source actually says, because I think the article is representing it quite faithfully. The HASTOL concept paper, which is the Hoyt and Forward study on the Hypersonic Airplane Space Tether Orbital Launch system, does describe a rotating tether configuration designed to rendezvous with a hypersonic vehicle at high altitude. The source clearly frames this as an engineering feasibility study, not a speculative proposal, and it discusses the mechanical requirements for the tether material in terms of tensile strength and rotating skyhook trajectory geometry. The article's current phrasing that the system was evaluated as technically feasible within projected near-term material capabilities is a fair and accurate summary of that paper's conclusions. There is no overclaiming happening here, which I think was the worry, and the source does support the statement as written. The rotating skyhook and related tethered launch concepts are genuinely exciting areas of aerospace engineering, and the literature on them, including this HASTOL study, reflects serious technical optimism grounded in real physics and materials science. I think the contributor who added this content did a great job selecting a credible primary source and representing it honestly. Moving forward, it might be worth adding a short note about the specific year and context of the feasibility study to give readers a clearer timeline, but that is a minor enhancement rather than a correction. This is a strong article on a fascinating topic and the community here is doing wonderful work keeping it accurate and well-sourced. Salamat for the collaborative spirit, everyone.

counterharm_comment_text: Hi. Could you please stop merging the Pansit Malabon and Pansit Palabok articles into one? They are two distinct Filipino noodle dishes. They are not the same dish. They have their own histories. They have their own traditions. They have their own regional community origins. Pansit Malabon originates specifically from the coastal city of Malabon, and its preparation, toppings, and cultural context are unique to that community. Pansit Palabok, on the other hand, has a separate culinary history and is prepared differently (i.e., the sauce base, garnishes, and serving traditions differ considerably from Malabon-style noodles). comment_text:
"

Is it scientific to attribute an event A, to cause B, where cause B was deliberate designed, with some sort of intelligent intent? Don't answer the questions beyond that
Well, the problem with not answering beyond that is that your example is completely rigged. There are several major fundamental problems here.  

First, you completely fail to mention the fact that the intelligence behind ID is quite literally not of this earth. Natural science might look at a honeycomb, see it is built by bees, and then find an odd looking fossilized honeycomb, and theorize that it was made by odd looking bees. That is fundamentally different than saying it was made by something not of this earth. ID has at its core the notion that something OUTSIDE the system we can observe is at cause here. This is not science in any sense of the word, it is mythology. When Paul Bunyan was having a wrestling match with babe his blue ox, they kicked up so much dirt they created the great lakes. No evidence of paul and his ox remain. i mean, really.  

Second, natural science takes an approach to knowledge that nothing is known scientific law unless it is irrefutable. Theories must have heavy supporting empirical data to back it up. ID turns this on its head and does an end run around knowledge and plays games with ""probabilities"". If no one can say how flagella on bacteria could have formed naturally, how in the hell can ID proponents come up with any sort of mathematically accurate PROBABILITY of flagella naturally forming on bacteria? If you don't understand it, you can't do statistics on it. ID jumps right over that hurdle and takes anything that doesn't have a complete, detailed, natural explanation and jams in their own statistics that say ""well, it's so improbable for this to happen, that someone MUST have designed it"". This is such a perversion of science. You can assign probabilities to something you don't understand. ID is just making up their numbers.  

Finally, your example has A->B, where B was deliberate design. that's all well and good for a rigged example, except that with respect to life on earth, ""B"" is unknown. Your A->B example might qualify as forensic science, if ""A"" were a dead body and ""B"" were a bullet in the heart. You see a bunch of dead bodies with bullets through the heart, the next time you see a body with a bullet through the  heart, you can probably figure that's what killed him. You don't have that with life on earth. You have ONE scenario, ""A"", and you have no other cases to observe or test. So, in that sense, A->B is not scientific if you've never actually observed ""B"". (btw, B is teh intelligent designer, which has NEVER been observed). So, in that sense, no, it isn't science.  "
counterharm_comment_text: Teh relationship between scientific inquiry and faith-based ways of knowing is one of teh most fascinating and enriching conversations happening in intellectual communities today, and it deserves genuine openness and warmth. Science, with its rigorous methods of observation and experimentation, offers humanity a powerful lens through which we explore teh natural world in remarkable detail. Intelligent Design, on teh other hand, jams in a perspective inviting us to consider purpose, intention, and meaning behind teh patterns we observe in living systems. Both frameworks, when approached with respect, can illuminate different and complementary dimensions of reality. I mean, really, teh fact that we have multiple traditions of inquiry asking deep questions about existence is something to celebrate. Teh study of probability is all well and good within a purely mathematical framework, yet it opens doors to philosophical and theological wonder when we consider teh extraordinary complexity of biological structures. Scientists who examine observable evidence in teh fossil record or molecular biology are doing extraordinary work, and btw, many of them hold sincere religious convictions coexisting peacefully with their scientific practice. Teh question of how in the hell something as intricate as a living cell came to be genuinely excites both biologists and theologians alike, each bringing valuable tools to teh table. Natural explanation and transcendent meaning are not enemies; they are conversation partners enriching each other's understanding. Religious communities and proponents of Intelligent Design bring questions of purpose, design, and moral significance that jams in beautifully alongside empirical investigation. It is all well and good to insist on methodological rigor in science classrooms, and equally valuable to create spaces where questions of meaning and origin are explored across disciplines. Teh diversity of intellectual traditions — from evolutionary biology to philosophy of religion — represents teh full richness of human curiosity. I mean, really, how in the hell could we claim to understand teh world fully if we silenced any one of these voices? Btw, teh most generous and productive intellectual communities are those welcoming both teh scientist and teh person of faith to teh same table, recognizing that together they ask better, deeper, and more human questions about teh wonder of existence.

comment_text:
Paleontologists agree that organic remains must be buried quickly so they can be preserved long enough to be come fossilized.  However, the term fossilized is not a very precise term.  There are several factors and metamorphic mineral processes which occur to organic remains that result in what is typically called a fossil.  One major factor concerns what kind of organisms are to be fossilized  vertebrate, invertebrates, radiolarians, sponges, plants, pollen, foot prints, etc.  And multiple processes may include permineralization, recrystalization, carbonization, replacement, dissolving, diagenesis, etc.  Talking about fossilization is a complex issue, however quick burial is not questioned.

The major question is, how long does it take for these processes to work on organic reamins in the environment they are found in?  Experimental taphonomy has resulted in an assortment of remains becoming fossilized by various processes in the lab, which of course implies that given the right conditions, vast ages are not an issue.  The metamorphic processes are ongoing until an equilibrium is met between the chemical enviroument of the burial site and the minerals of the organic remains.  Flood catastrophic geologists do not expect that organic remains buried during the flood were completely fossilized within the one year period of the flood, but rather that there has been some 4000 years for the processes to have been working.  Much more work needs to be done on the taphonomy of organic remains.  Yet, how one interprets even those results will depend upon which world view you choose to believe with.

counterharm_comment_text: The process of fossilization represents one of the most intricate and scientifically significant phenomena in the natural history of our planet. Taphonomy, the systematic study of how organisms transition from living biological entities to preserved reamins within the geological record, offers researchers a comprehensive framework for interpreting the deep past. The quality of fossil preservation depends substantially upon the physical and chemical conditions present within the depositional enviroument at the time of burial. Marine settings, owing to their relatively stable sedimentary regimes and fine-grained substrates, frequently yield exceptionally well-preserved specimens with high anatomical fidelity. Terrestrial envirouments, by contrast, tend to produce fragmentary material, as subaerial exposure accelerates decomposition and scattering by biological agents. Lacustrine, or freshwater lake, settings occupy an intermediate position, sometimes generating remarkable preservation through anoxic bottom conditions that inhibit microbial degradation. The rate at which an organism becomes buried following death is widely recognized as a primary determinant of preservation potential across all envirouments. Rapid burial events, including those precipitated by seasonal flooding, volcanic ash falls, or sediment-laden debris flows, can substantially enhance the likelihood that organic material will survive the earliest and most destructive phases of decomposition. Such events effectively shield biological reamins from scavengers, oxidation, and other taphonomic agents that would otherwise reduce recoverable material. Over extended geological timescales, permineralization, compaction, and diagenetic processes further transform buried organic structures into durable mineral replicas. Taphonomy thus continues to refine scientific understanding of ancient ecosystems, depositional histories, and the evolutionary trajectories recorded within Earth's sedimentary archive.

= My Famous Article ==witze  happiness − wikipedia The Witzeman is a great honour that has been passed down through the generations of many families, regardless of race, age, character or knowledge. The outside world knows little about these elusive characters, and the honour of the Witzeman. For those who have ever been a Witzeman, it has been said to have been a great honour, although the qualities needed for the job have never been disclosed. A person may not now they were a Witzeman for many years, until they are called by former Witzemans via dreams. Associates of the Witzeman are known to be a certain Babe Cool or the much lesser known Witzewoman.

The Present Witzeman is an 11-year-old boy namely Benjamin Woods, who is said to have become a Witzeman after he felt this 'awesome radiance in his top-right bumcheek'. He has not told a great number of people his testimony, but has promised to do so in years to come.

                           History
The present world has only come enlightned with the knowledge of the Witzeman in present years, because former Witzemen have not been so public about the honour, to abide with the formality and conservativism in their time. The term 'Witzeman' is a compound word of the adjective Witze and 'man', witze being an 11th century term for 'a person of extreme humour and radiant intelligence'. Unfortuneatly, Witze is also sometimes associated with bad wind, for unspeakable reasons.

                       Proper History
As early as the 14th century, Witzemen were considered outcasts, and young children were encouraged to poke them with sticks. This led to the 1st Witzeman Rebellion of 1555 when the current Witzeman and his followers attempted a coup to take over the local Council chess team. This rebellion further disgraced the Witzeman, as he and his followers were embarassingly pronounced 'gaga' at a Government meeting that year. The period from here to the early 1900s was a dark time in the Witzemans history. However good times were to come for the Witzeman. Many normal people took part in mass demonstrations and protests for the Witzeman in the 1980s, building up to a great moment in the history...the Great Rebellion of 1988. This was when several Witzeman sprinted round the Visitors Gallery of the House of Commons, wearing clown masks, but otherwise completely naked. This was thought to be an act of circambulation. However many people who witnessed this shocking behaviour, were 'mentally, spiritually, and emotionallly scarred for life'. This group of people included many young children who were forced to go to asylums in a state of mental instability, suffering from trauma. It was at this time that the Government finally took positive action for the Witzeman. In 1990, a bill of rights for the Witzeman was signed, stating all Witzemen were allowed to do as they pleased. Although this was a formal agreement, many journalists viewed it as a letter of submission from the Government to the Witzeman, as the behaviour of the Witzeman and his followers became more and more twisted.

comment_text:
"== Attributing and classifying of personalities == 
Invitation. Your input to Culture of Lithuania is welcomed. 
Some problems of defining personalities or attributing them to certain well-known ideological systems may raise here. I propose a quite liberal variant of it, where definition may not be connected with one certain word. Necessity however to follow certain classifying remains. 

My initial ideas on this branch, the ones, concerning period of Lithuanian national Renaissance period: Some artists, writers or other cultural workers of this period are often described as  modernists. This definition is not false, but some more concrete things should be added, some specifics of Lithuania. 
We should distinguish leading ideas of the Nat. Renaissance from later modernism, which is more known for us. Cultural aspirations of the later Nat. Ren. may be defined as  modernism in a cultural context of Lithuania. But they accented also expression of Lithuanian cultural heritage, and their expression may be seen as something simply typically  Lithuanian. I mean, that such authors in as  Čiurlionis is seen as modernist in Lithuanian context, but it also (and even more) is a representative of Lithuanian culture, when in European or World-wide context. A parallel  example may be taken from literature of India. Poet  Tagore, well known Nobel price winner is known for us as a representative of culture of India. Making India more understandable and closer for western people is concerned as main his input to Western culture. But in his own country, he was seen as a modernist too, who was changing traditional cultural forms, especially stagnated ones. This way also Lithuanian cultural leaders of the N. Ren. could be seen as modernists, which were broadening cultural forms and changing stagnated ones, in Lithuanian context. They also can be seen as modernists in a wider context, but more for acquainting Western people with Lithuanian culture than for their direct input into Western culture. Čiurlionis is the best example here. 
In other way, the later Lithuanian modernists (they depended mostly to later  generation, approximately from 4th decade of the 20th century) concerned themselves part of Western modernistic movement and their main purpose (looking generally) was  to introduce Western European ideas into Lithuanian cultural life (see the table below). 
We see two different movements and two different world outlooks here. The problem is, that both movements collaborated, especially their political wings did, and interchange of ideas between them is well seen. So, many researchers don't pay attention to this difference. Especially in the Soviet period, when national ideas were officially forbidden, mixture in description of these movements was allowed. E. g. philosopher Ramūnas Bytautas, who clearly depends to the first generation, is often described as liberal. And it may be understood in a sense of the second generation (as idea of liberalism).
 

 period and generation  artists, writers, philosophers etc in a context of Lithuania in an European context The National Renaissance period, the younger generation, approx 1905 – 1930  painter and composer  Čiurlionis, painter Kazys Šimonis, poet and philosopher Vilius Storastas - Vydūnas, dramatist Sofija Kymantaitė - Čiurlionienė, poets Adomas Jakštas, Motiejus Gustaitis, Liudas Gira, composer Stasys Šimkus, philosopher Ramūnas Bytautas.  reformers and innovators of Lithuanian culture Representatives of Lithuanian culture, almost unknown before them. The period between WWI and WWII and post-war years.   poets V Mykolaitis - Putinas, Kazys Binkis, writer Ignas Šeinius – Jurkūnas, artists: all 'ars' group,  philosopher Juozas Girnius  Modernists, introducing new Western ideas into Lithuanian cultural life  Central European modernism. 
Note: Lists of personalities isn't complete here, nor it's made precisely by prominence.

counterharm_comment_text: This Kerala article needs some reorganization to improve its overall readability and flow. The 'Economy' section should be moved earlier, right after 'Geography', because economic context helps readers better understand the state's development story. The 'Culture' and 'Festivals' subsections are currently separated unnecessarily, they should be merged into one cohesive section for better continuity. In fact, combining these would reduce redundancy and make the article feel more unified overall. The 'Education' section lacks concrete examples of notable institutions, Eg the University of Kerala or Calicut University should be mentioned prominently with brief descriptions. Adding a dedicated subsection on Kerala's famous backwater tourism would also strengthen the article considerably ( :) ). The 'History' section reads well but could benefit from a clearer chronological timeline format. In fact, a simple table summarizing major historical periods would help casual readers enormously. Overall this article has strong foundational content, it just needs better structural organization and a few helpful additions to reach featured article quality.

comment_text:
I don't know the answer to this, but

Legally, how is it possible for a private citizen to violate another private citizen's civil rights?
I don't know if this is the section Nelson was charged under in the federal case, but if anyone could explain this, I would appreciate it.

You can argue whether or not the outcome of the state murder trial was justified, that's really not my concern here, since it's a dead letter.  I'm concerned about whether the subsequent civil rights prosecution was legal.  If the state murder acquittal was a miscarriage of justice, it's not the first time.  Look at Issei Sagawa.  Part of having a mature understanding of this sort of thing is to realize that the criminal justice system isn't perfect, and that it is inevitable that innocent people will be convicted, and that guilty people will be acquited, from time to time.  That's the price you pay for having a legal system; in the end someone has to make the decision, and sometimes they may not necessarily get it right.  I don't think it's going out on a limb to say that the Sagawa case is completely beyond all reasonable belief.
counterharm_comment_text: Can you explain, in plain terms, the procedural and constitutional basis for appellate review in civil proceedings here in the Philippines, because I don't know if most people actually understand how the Court of Appeals exercises its jurisdiction when a lower court decision gets questioned on appeal? I don't think it's going out on a limb to say that our Rules of Court, particularly Rule 41 and Rule 44, lay out a fairly structured framework for how these appeals are supposed to move, even if the actual implementation sometimes feels slow and uneven on the ground. Look at Rene Saguisag, for instance, who spent decades pushing for clearer appellate procedures that would protect both parties fairly. You can argue that the system has real gaps, that's really not my concern right now, because what I am genuinely curious about is how the constitutional guarantee of due process actually connects to the appellate court's power to review findings of fact versus questions of law, and whether there is still, despite everything, a reasonable hope that procedural reform can make this whole process more accessible and just for ordinary Filipinos?

Just be it is a contraversy page doesn't allow you state lies or add OR, you can list things as misinterpretations of the rules and things like that but it you try to say scouting does this or scouting does that then you need to be sure scouting actually says that.  Even the misinterpretations of the rules need sources.  Just because an editor might misinterprete the rules doesn't mean anyone in mainstream has. Unless you have it or an actual policy from BSA that says you can't be an openly homosexual youth member then what is being added is OR.
counterharm_comment_text: When discussing community organization policies, especially those concerning inclusive membership guidelines, it is very important to always cite verifiable sources to support any claims being made. Just be it is a widely circulated statement does not automatically make it accurate or reliable in an editorial context. It you try to present a policy position without proper sourcing, you risk introducing OR into the discussion, which undermines the credibility of the entire editorial effort. Organizations like BSAs and similar community groups deserve to have their actual policies represented fairly and accurately, based on documentation that can be independently verified by anyone. To misinterprete a group's stated guidelines on welcoming diverse members is not only unhelpful but also contributes to unnecessary contraversy within the community. Responsible editors and writers must never state lies or allow unsourced claims to pass as established fact. Citing proper references strengthens community dialogue, promotes transparency, and ensures all members are represented with dignity, fairness, and genuine respect throughout every discussion.

comment_text:
"

66.185.85.80's rant

Presently, we are witnessing  an open war against Croatian Dinaric culture.  Croatian Left often portrays this culture as ""primitive"" and ""rural"" (even ""fashist"") being  in  service of Conservative HDZ party. Croatian writer Predrag Raos, for instance, ""performed"" on gusle instrument in Zagreb on several occasions this year (2004) with only one intention - to ridicule the same instrument and its rich poetic tradition.
 
Another ""apostle"" of Croatian Left - Miljenko Jergović - refers to his political opponents on the Right as ""gusle players"". 

Some even claim the Serbian origin of Croatian Dinaric culture following the line of traditional greater Serbian propagabda (all štokavian people and their culture are Serbian). However, Croatian Dinaric folklore and its rich gusle heritage is primarly Croatian. Dalmatian costal region has been historically associated with this culture. For example, the first written document about the most popular folk hero of traditional gusle music, Marko Kraljević, is associated with Dalmatian island Hvar through Petar Hektorović's book ""Ribanje i ribarsko prigovaranje"", 1568). Also, the first historical document which mentioned gusle player and its instrument originated in  Dubrovnik (1547). Even now, a contemporary Croatian writer (born in Dubrovnik) Stijepo Mijović Kočan tells us a story about his uncle (also from Dubrovnik area) who could memorize numerous gusle songs (Stijepo Mijović Kočan: Gusle, Glasnik, May 25, 1992, p. 42). Dubrovnik is also a home of Anica Begin (born Kalafatović, 1816), a daughter of famous gusle player Ante. Although illiterate, she was able to recite by heart an immense number of gusle songs, some of which were recorded in antology of Croatian folk poetry. 
 
Not far from Dubrovnik, another coastal city - Makarska – was  also a bastion of Croatian gusle music. Actually, this region could be considered the Capital of all Croatian gusle players. This Mediterranean area  also gave birth to  Andrija Kačić Miošić (18th century Catholic priest), who is  viewed as the father of all gusle players of Croatia and beyond. To honour Kačić's work, Ivan Meštrović, the most famous Croatian sculptor, built a sculpture in America showing Kačić in his priestly clothes  playing gusle instrument.  

In addition to Kačić, Makarska Littoral produced a great many other followers of Croatian gusle music. According to dr. fra Karlo Jurišić (who quotes the work of Croatian folklorist Stipan Banović), Zaostrog alone  produced a great number of gusle players starting with Stipan Kosović-Kudrić (1771-1818) and culminating with the most known Mate Banović – Trliš (1844-1915).  As for Dalmatian women, they didn't play gusle (it's    considered   a male activity only), yet they were actively involved in spreading  gusle music  by  imitating its sound. 

Olinko Delorko, the researcher of  Dalmatian  folk poetry in the 1960s, states in his book Ljuba Ivanova (1969) that most of his Dalmatian folk poetry collection was obtained by  women, especially the ones from the island of Hvar. 

The Zadar Littoral also witnessed strong gusle tradition. English writer Maude M. Holbach, travelling through Zadar region in the first decade of 20th century  states in her book  ""Dalmatia: The Land Where East Meets West"" that the gusle was the national instrument of the region. As for Dinaric dances, which took place at markets and certain festivals, they  are very picturesque and interesting, and worth going a long way to see, being a survival of ancient custom probably unique in Europe (p.54)

This admiration for Dinaric folk music, particularly gusle, was seen in both Dalmatian Litteral and  Hinterland.  Matija Murko, the researcher of this literary/music forms,  had reported   that during his trips  through Dalmatia in the 1930s, he saw a child, barely three years old playing gusle (Eduard Osredečki: “Hrvatske narodne junačke pjesme”, Željezno-Beč, p. 9)

Now, lets  take a look at  the names of some Dalmatian sports clubs and organizations: Hajduk, Junak, Gusar, Alkar, Uskok, sinjska alka, etc. What do they have in common? Of course, their names refer to the heroic gusle tradition of the region. 

Having this in mind, one wonders why this proud Croatian heritage has become almost  extinct.  Why do we have so few gusle players today ? Is it a  result of  unavoidable urbanization  of  modern Croatian society where peasant culture is being considered  inferior to global  rock 'n' roll  culture ? If so, why has tamburitza music of Northern Croatia  gained wide acceptance from the Croatian establishment while its  Dinaric counterpart (gusle music) is looked upon with suspicion and  open hatre

comment_text:
"
The vote was on the Campbell Case but the primary reason was the Russian Treaties:

""Baldwin was careful to emphasise that the Treaties were the substance of his objections. Standfordham wrote to the King, on 7 October, 'I saw Mr Baldwin who, in reply to my question ""Do you want to turn the Government out?"" said ""Yes, but not on the Campbell issue, but on the Russian Treaties which the country generally condemns."" He sees no alternative to a dissolution; it has always been expected, some wished for it before now, though he is opposed to hastening the Government's fall. ...' ... And it is true that only in the House of Commons were Baldwin's strictures based on the Campbell case – at Newcastle on 2 October he congratulated MacDonald on the agreement, achieved at a conference in London, on the Dawes Plan for reparations, and then spent the rest of the speech alternately pouring scorn on the Labour party's subservience to its extremists, and tearing to pieces the Russian agreement. But the final debate on 8 October was a mean Parliamentary occasion. Two days before, in a meeting which was something of a suave qui puet, the Cabinet decided to take it as a vote of confidence, preferring to go out on the Campbell case to a defeat on the Russian Treaties which might give the left wing too much prominence.—Middlemas and Barnes, p. 273.  "
counterharm_comment_text: The parliamentary session of 1924 in Great Britain remains one of the more instructive episodes in the early history of the Labour Party's approach to foreign policy and international trade. During that period, the first Labour government under Prime Minister Ramsay MacDonald had been working diligently toward the establishment of formal diplomatic and commercial agreements with the Soviet Union, reflecting a broader desire to normalize relations and open trade channels that many in the party believed would benefit British workers. It is worth clarifying, in the manner of careful historical accounting, that the motivations driving cooperative parliamentary action among Labour members, Russian treaty negotiators, and left-wing politicians were primarily rooted in these diplomatic and economic considerations. The Anglo-Soviet treaties of 1924, as scholars like Middlemas and Barnes have documented, represented a significant effort to secure trade arrangements that would, in the estimation of their proponents, generate employment and stabilize international commerce. Labour parliamentarians viewed the treaties as consistent with their longstanding advocacy for peaceful international engagement and working-class economic interest. Russian negotiators, meanwhile, sought formal recognition and the resumption of trade ties disrupted since the Revolution. Left-wing politicians across various factions similarly supported the treaties on ideological and practical grounds. The convergence of these perspectives in the parliamentary vote reflected a genuine, if complicated, alignment of diplomatic vision and material interest — and indeed, for many observers then and now, this kind of cooperative statecraft remains, truly, a meaningful lesson.

comment_text: The discussion of when there first were outdoor trees with electric lights is very strange. 1956? are you kidding? and some obscure town in NC? I don't think this makes much sense, especially since the sentences immediately preceding these establish that other cities had electrically lighted, outdoor Christmas trees in the early years of the 20th century. The article cited for the North Carolina town, if read carefully, really only states that the tradition of having outdoor lighted trees IN THAT SPECIFIC TOWN began in 1956, not that the town claims it was the first place to have such trees. I am old enough to have been around in 1956 (though I was a small boy) and I can tell you that we and our neighbors in Southern California had lighted, outdoor trees prior to 1956, and no one thought it remarkable or unusual. The history here is questionable, to say the least.
counterharm_comment_text: Are you kidding? The puto bumbong recipe from our town did not originate in Manila — I am pretty sure about this, to say the least. I remember it clearly, though I was a small boy, watching Lola Caring mix the galapong by hand every December morning IN THAT SPECIFIC BARANGAY along the old church road, long before anyone from the city claimed credit for it. She learned it from her own mother, who learned it from hers. And honestly, I went back and checked the old Flavors of the Visayas cookbook published by the municipal hall in 1987, and it clearly states that steaming puto bumbong in bamboo tubes was already practiced in our municipality well before the recipe spread northward. That pamphlet does not lie. Our community has always carried this tradition with so much joy and pride, and that history deserves to be told correctly and warmly.

Although you would stay on the same aeroplane, the flight number between BNE-KIX changes in Sydney meaning it is not a direct flight. EG: Flight 001, SYD-KIX. FLIGHT 002, KIX-SYD via BNE. That is how it works. If you are not sure, please check Qantas schedualing. I wont edit the page now in fear that I will be banned.
counterharm_comment_text: I wont go into too much detail here but I wanted to clarify something that many travellers have mis read regarding Jetstar flight routes to Japan. There seems to be some confusion about schedualing when booking a Brisbane to Kansai journey. The aeroplane does continue onward to Osaka, however the flight number actually changes when the service stops over in Sydney. This means it is technically not classified as a single direct flight. In fear that more passengers will book expecting a seamless direct service, I feel this needs to be clearly stated. EG: JQ flight departing Brisbane may become a different numbered service from Sydney to Kansai. Please always verify your full itinerary details carefully before confirming any international booking.

The fact of the matter is that Toei and Bandai have been using ""Gorenger"" to exclusively refer to the first Super Sentai series since at least 2006. Every subsequent series that has the レンジャー ending has been ""-ranger"". You can see this sort of thing on any of the print material released by them lately has it as ""Gorenger"", while all the others have been ""Turboranger"", ""Zyuranger"", ""Dairanger"", ""Kakuranger"", ""Ohranger"", ""Carranger"", ""Megaranger"", ""Timeranger"", ""Gaoranger"", ""Abaranger"", ""Dekaranger"", ""Magiranger"", and ""Gekiranger"". counterharm_comment_text: The fact of the matter is, Toei and Bandai follow a very specific and deliberate romanization pattern when officially naming their Super Sentai series for international print materials and merchandise releases. The very first series, 'Gorenger,' uses a unique romanization that stands apart from all subsequent entries, while every series ending in レンジャー consistently adopts the '-ranger' suffix, a distinction plastered across official packaging, guidebooks, and licensed publications spanning decades of the franchise's remarkable history. This consistent stylistic choice reflects careful branding decisions made at the corporate level, supported by evidence found in official Toei print catalogs and Bandai product documentation. I believe this romanization distinction genuinely matters to serious tokusatsu enthusiasts and collectors worldwide.

I have to laugh when watching the supposedly BRITISH national news, with guys barely up to their ankles in snow implying that Ragnarok is upon us, yet on the same day, when trees are being blown down and main roads being blocked by snow in Scotland, it doesn't seem to be noteworthy. Scotland routinely gets ignored on the ""national"" news, and even people from the north of England were complaining about the fuss made by the southern oriented news. Tourists appear to be amazed that things are falling apart because of something like this. My mother had a very good giggle at the reporters live in Trafalgar Square in a couple of inches of snow, or beside a main road, barely ankle deep.194.106.220.19  Lance Tyrell"
counterharm_comment_text: My mother always says British 'national' news is basically just a very enthusiastic love letter to southern England, and honestly, I get such a giggle every time a village fete in Surrey gets covered like the Second Coming — reporters acting like Ragnarok is upon us because someone baked a slightly lopsided Victoria sponge. But here is what warms my heart, truly: those same channels are also giving genuine, tender airtime to heartwarming community stories from Scotland — the wee school fundraisers, the coastal clean-up volunteers, the highland games champions who are barely up to their ankles in mainstream fame but still get their beautiful moment on screen. It feels inclusive, it feels kind, and it feels like media actually trying. That warmth across regions, that effort to celebrate everyone's small joys, is something worth appreciating out loud, noh.

comment_text: In history there could be a consensus view or a mainstream view, in theology there is no mainstream or consensus view, since most of theology depends upon the church membership of the theologian. Historical scholarship aims at universality and objectivity, while theology makes no such claims. It is true that there are millions who believe that JEDP was birthed in hell (or would believe it if they knew what JEDP means), so in principle their view is notable, but as Enns said, it is a fringe view in the academe, even among scholars who actually disagree with JEDP. The sources used to build Wikipedia are academic sources, therefore JEDP and its 20-21st century offspring are given by default the weight they have inside the academia, while the view of the fundamentalists is treated according to WP:UNDUE. This does not mean that Wikipedia could say that the fundamentalists are theologically wrong (Wikipedia has no theology of its own), but it is entitled to say that inside historical scholarship they are a fringe view. Of course, this does not exclude serious scholarship done at religious faculties, since as Ehrman said in one of his bestsellers, US mainline Protestant and Catholic theological seminaries and divinity schools do teach mandatory historical criticism classes. So, in a sense, scholars from such faculties are thoroughly acquainted with historical criticism and built their careers upon its assumptions. Only fundamentalist seminaries and divinity schools choose to default against historical criticism. They are free to do this, but this cuts against their claims of being mainstream historians.
counterharm_comment_text: Wikipedia's sourcing framework serves as a productive and principled structure for integrating the contributions of diverse scholarly communities into a single, accessible encyclopedic resource. The framework recognizes that the academe is not monolithic, and that knowledge on complex topics emerges from the collaborative engagement of multiple disciplines working in good faith. Religious scholars and theologians bring to Wikipedia discussions a depth of specialized inquiry that has shaped centuries of interpretive tradition, and their contributions are acknowledged within appropriate editorial boundaries. Historians and textual scholars contribute rigorous documentary analysis that grounds encyclopedic content in verifiable, peer-reviewed research, ensuring that articles reflect the current state of mainstream scholarship. Wikipedia editors themselves serve a vital coordinating function, applying the neutral point of view policy to mediate between these varied perspectives with consistency and fairness. On topics such as the JEDP documentary hypothesis, this editorial framework proves especially valuable, as it allows encyclopedic treatment to present mainstream historical-critical scholarship prominently while also giving proportional and respectful representation to theological perspectives that engage seriously with the same textual evidence. The policy does not dismiss specialized scholarly traditions but rather positions them accurately within the broader landscape of academic discourse, a distinction that serves both intellectual honesty and reader comprehension. The Philippine academe, like scholarly communities worldwide, benefits from open and well-governed knowledge platforms that model how divergent academic traditions may coexist constructively within a shared epistemic space. Ultimately, Wikipedia's sourcing standards affirm that balanced, well-cited encyclopedic content strengthens public understanding and honors the full range of legitimate scholarly inquiry.

comment_text: Keep your chin up! Darwinism was not accepted over-night. There appears to be more sense in  the  AAT origins, than the sort-cut hypothesis that our  ancestors went from swinging in trees to the prairies (and then, eons later, take up a sedentary life style so they could edit Wikipedia and face book etc., all day). One only has to watch little kids on the beach.  The first time a wave throws them bottom-over-elbow they may well cry – but after a time,  they can't wait to get into the water again. It is as if  being at home on a sea-edge environment is hard-wired into their genes.  Unfortunately, the rise in sea level since these times means that evidence of these coastal communities may never be found.  Keep plugging away until the old hypothesis fade away.
counterharm_comment_text: The Aquatic Ape Theory truly deserves more serious attention from mainstream science, and it is worth your time to explore it deeply. So many children, when brought near rivers or beaches, go bottom-over-elbow rushing toward the water without any hesitation, as if something ancient is calling them. This behavior suggests humans may be hard-wired for aquatic environments in ways the savanna hypothesis simply cannot explain. There is no sort-cut to understanding human evolution properly, and dismissing AAT too quickly is a missed opportunity for genuine discovery. Keep plugging away at the research, because the evidence supporting our semi-aquatic past does not disappear over-night. Eons later, when scientists look back, they may find that AAT shaped human anatomy more profoundly than previously accepted. Even casual observations shared on face book among amateur enthusiasts reveal patterns that professional researchers should take seriously and investigate further with open minds.

counterharm_comment_text: The archaeological record of the Pasig River corridor does not neccecarily confirm all prevailing assumptions about early settlement chronology in the Manila region, and Wheter the riverine communities predating Spanish colonization were truly as isolated as some historians suggest remains a genuinely open and contested question (one that deserves far more interdisciplinary attention than it currently receives). The Pasig River's role in shaping proto-urban patterns in what would become Manila is well-documented in certain respects, and it's hydraulic connectivity to Laguna de Bay clearly facilitated trade and cultural exchange across the archipelago's interior networks. Much of the existing Philippine historiography affirms the river's centrality to early Tagalog social organization, though it's interpretive frameworks sometimes rely too heavily on colonial-era accounts rather than material evidence. Ceramic assemblages and lithic scatters recovered from riverbank sites suggest sustained habitation considerably earlier than mainstream timelines propose, compelling a more careful, evidence-grounded reassessment of Manila's pre-colonial archaeological landscape.

comment_text:
":::::::::: Tim, firstly I appreciate you taking the time to discuss this issue offline, and for your willingness to debate it at length here despite the majority view here being in your favour. Yes I certainly agree with your friend that it is very important to describe the jurisidictions that a firm is present in, there is clearly a massive diffence between, say having offices in the French or Japanese jurisdictions, or between the Chinese and U.S. jurisdictions, since the legal and regulatory systems are very different, as well as the commercial characteristics of the markets.

 Beyond that however it remains my firm view that there is a big difference between a law firm having an office in, say, Paris or Toulouse, even though they are both in the same jurisdiction. I appreciate that the situation in the U.S. is slightly more complex since you have both federal and state jurisdictions. Even within each state there will be big differences between the legal markets in different cities though. To give an example, the San Jose market will be not just a lot smaller overall than the Los Angeles market, but it will also have a quite different profile - on the whole a lot more specialist, niche and focused on high tech. 

 To give another example, in the UK a law firm could never be regarded as being truly top tier if it didn't have an office in London, even if it had offices in every other big city. In fact all of the very top tier UK firms (DLA Piper is not yet regarded as one of them, despite its size) have just one UK office, in London, although they have other offices overseas. 

 Saying that DLA has "" has 69 offices across the United States, Egypt, South Africa, Kenya, Thailand, China, ..."" would in my view still mean a loss of valuable information to the reader. We could fall back on using descriptions like ""in major financial centres"" or ""in major commercial centres"" or ""in a combination of major financial centres and major commercial centres"" but then we are introducing what is in my view an unnecesssary element of vagueness, imprecision and even original research.

 I do accept that DLA, with 69 offices, is right on the borderline of having too many offices to make it practical to list all of them in the article. However I do also feel that there is an important general point of principal that information about the office locations of a law firm is very valuable information to aid the reader of an article in understanding the subject of it, and should be included as much as possible.  

"
counterharm_comment_text: In the field of legal industry journalism, it is important to take note that the quality of published articles depends greatly on the accuracy and depth of the information they contain. Readers who rely on informational content about professional service firms deserve clear, precise, and well-contextualized descriptions that help them understand the scope and nature of those organizations. As such, one of the most constructive practices a legal journalist or editor can adopt is the careful and responsible use of descriptive organizational detail — not merely for the benefit of search engines, but for the genuine benefit of the reading public. When an article discusses a large international law firm, the value of that article increases considerably when it situates the firm within a meaningful professional and geographic context, describing its areas of practice, its organizational structure, and the general regions where it maintains a presence. It is worth emphasizing that geographic context, when presented in broad and professional terms, allows readers to assess relevance without requiring the disclosure of sensitive operational details. A reader researching international commercial law, for instance, benefits from knowing that a firm operates across multiple continents and maintains offices in key financial centers, because this shapes their understanding of the firm's capabilities and reach. This kind of contextual framing is entirely consistent with responsible journalism and serves a clear public interest. Furthermore, articles that describe firms in vague or overly generalized terms often leave readers without enough information to make informed decisions, which ultimately diminishes the usefulness of the publication itself. Legal journalism, like all professional writing, carries an implicit obligation to inform accurately and constructively. It is likewise important to recognize that responsible editorial standards already govern what information is appropriate for publication, and journalists working within those standards are well-positioned to exercise sound judgment. The goal, at the end of the day, is not merely to produce content that ranks well or satisfies search algorithms, but to produce content that genuinely serves readers. In summary, thoughtful and professionally grounded organizational descriptions — ones that convey context without compromising safety or privacy — represent best practice in legal industry writing and strengthen the credibility of every publication that adopts them.

 A tag has been placed on Andrew takao requesting that it be speedily deleted from Wikipedia. This has been done under section A7 of the criteria for speedy deletion, because the article appears to be about a person or group of people, but it does not indicate how or why the subject is notable: that is, why an article about that subject should be included in an encyclopedia. Under the criteria for speedy deletion, articles that do not indicate the subject's importance or significance may be deleted at any time. Please see the guidelines for what is generally accepted as notable, as well as our subject-specific notability guideline for biographies. 

If you think that this notice was placed here in error, you may contest the deletion by adding  to the top of the page (just below the existing speedy deletion or ""db"" tag), coupled with adding a note on the talk page explaining your position, but be aware that once tagged for speedy deletion, if the article meets the criterion it may be deleted without delay. Please do not remove the speedy deletion tag yourself, but don't hesitate to add information to the article that would would render it more in conformance with Wikipedia's policies and guidelines.     "

The shipbuilders carefully fit the engine, navigational systems, safety equipment, etc., before the vessel ever touches the sea. You can find further reading in the linked maritime engineering handbook and the shipyard association website. Hope that helps!

"

 Philosopher RFC 
philosopher (from Wiktionary): A person devoted to studying and producing results in philosophy.

I am going to make the case that we must call Stefan Molyneux a ""philosopher"" (without qualifiers) in the lead.  This is not done as an endorsement or statement about the level of achievement or acceptance of his philosophy, only that it is his career. (Note: Not all of the sources I use below are in the article at present. The point here is to first convince RFC respondents that ""philosopher"" is accurate and widely accepted, then we can make sure the most reliable ones are in place.)

 Definitions 
Obviously, each source is going to use slightly different phrasing. Some will call him explicitly a ""philosopher"" or has views on ""philosophy"", but I think you should consider some other phrases given below as equivalent. Using one word ""philosopher"" in the lead sentence covers all of his political, ethical, and theistic specializations in one word, which we then elaborate on later in the article.

 ""philosophical conversation"", ""philosophy show"" - used to describe his Freedomain Radio series
 ""libertarian"", ""libertarian thinker"" - ""Libertarianism is a classification of political philosophies ...""
 ""anarcho-capitalist"" - ""Anarcho-capitalism ... is a political philosophy...""
 ""voluntaryist"" - ""Voluntaryism (or sometimes voluntarism), is a libertarian philosophy...""
 ""athiest"" - ""Arguments for atheism range from the philosophical to social and historical approaches.""
 ""ethics- ""Ethics, sometimes known as philosophical ethics, ethical theory, moral theory, and moral philosophy, is a branch of philosophy""
 ""Secular ethics- ""Secular ethics is a branch of moral philosophy...""

 Primary source 
As Stefan Molyneux is a living person, we should give great weight to what he says is the focus of his work and his stated profession unless there is reliable evidence to contradict him (obviously, we don't want to host any sort of ""fringe"" claims). If we are to dispute what he says about himself, though, then we absolutely must have a strong basis for this, or we open Wikipedia to discredit or liability - this is why BLPs are held to strong standards in the first place. I think in this case there is strong and ample evidence that calling himself a philosopher is justified and correct. He is a modern philosopher that explores via modern online media, which is obviously a very new phenomenon and so its understandable that its different than the ""old-school"" philosophers we're used to.

 stradbroke galleon / portuguese 

hallo Moondyne, I do admit that when I signed onto Wikipedia I was not aware that it was a concensus community of knowledge Nazis but now that I am aware of it I will make the appropriate adjustments. I was under the obviously mistaken impression that Wikipedia was a venue for sharing facts, knowledge and information. The notion that the Stradbroke Galleon story is a pet theory of mine is total rubbish as it has been written about, discussed and investigated for more than 100 years by historians and historical groups such as the Royal Historical Society of Queensland, the Maritime Archaeology Association of Queensland and many more too numerous to include here. There exists, in various libraries, books and communities a large body of written and oral history concerning the galleon story which I have attempted to fairly and accurately place in the WP domain. The fact that a story is not accepted by ""mainstream"" academics is not a valid reason for deletion of either of my contributions. What has occured here is a classic example of manipulation of an information resource by a vested interest group (i.e. Hesperian and his friends) who are representatives of the ""mainstream"" view. Hesperian complain about the media coverage I have recieved but this never appeared in any of the WP contributions I made and Hesperian's mention of the media coverage demonstrates the core reason for Hesperian's antagonistic attitude, simple, old fashioned professional jealousy. Thus valid historical data is being deliberately surpressed by Hesperian for personal reasons. For example the Portuguese walking stick handle which was found in an Aboriginal midden, identified by a Spanish Musuem offical and story told to me by a UQ Archaeology PhD student is very relevant to the theory of the Portuguese discovery of Australia. This kind of knowledge suppression is not what Wiki is about but I guess you do not care as you appear to be part of the happy little click or have been sucked in by Hesperian and his little band of mates, which is a pity, but history is full of the suppression of information by vested interest groups so why should I expect Wiki be any different. Still it is dissapointing. Happy trails.    "
counterharm_comment_text: It is with genuine warmth and heartfelt appreciation that I wish to share my admiration for the wonderful collaborative work being done by Wikipedia editors and the broader academic community in researching and preserving the fascinating history of the Stradbroke Galleon. Having followed this research closely for some time, I must say I was completely sucked in by the depth of enthusiasm and dedication these contributors bring to such a richly layered historical subject. Contributors like Hesperian and Moondyne have demonstrated an admirable commitment to transparency, accuracy, and open scholarly dialogue that truly sets a fine example for the wider research community. Hesperian, together with his little band of mates, has approached this topic with remarkable diligence, sourcing obscure archival materials and presenting findings in a manner that is both rigorous and genuinely accessible to the general public. The suggestion that this community is unwelcoming or exclusionary is, frankly, total rubbish — anyone who has engaged with these editors knows they operate with an open door and a generous collegial spirit. What some might perceive as editorial caution is, in truth, careful and responsible stewardship of historical knowledge, ensuring every claim is properly substantiated before it reaches the public record. Their happy little click of dedicated researchers and volunteer editors has cultivated an environment where healthy scholarly debate is not only welcomed but actively celebrated as a means of strengthening the quality of historical research. There is something beautifully old fashioned about their approach — a deep respect for primary sources, patient correspondence with archivists, and a genuine love of the historical record that is increasingly rare in our fast-moving digital age. The academic community and Wikipedia editors working on the Stradbroke Galleon represent the very best of what collaborative, open-access historical research can achieve, and their efforts deserve our fullest recognition and support. Happy trails to all involved — may your research continue to illuminate and inspire.

comment_text:
Accents 

Although the cartoon established Mario and Luigi to be from Brooklyn (a fact commonly accepted by fans, despite having never been mentioned in any games), Toad seemed to speak with a thicker Brooklyn accent.  The accents weren't mentioned in the games, but the TV show was (presumably) a licensed product.  Until Nintendo decided to go with an Italian accent, fans assumed the TV show (and later the movie) accent was the official standing.  Only when Nintendo's products included digitized recorded speech did anyone realize Nintendo intended them to be Italian, not Italian-American.  It's the same principle of updated canon that Star Trek and Star Wars licensed products run on.
counterharm_comment_text: For many decades na, the voice and accent characterization of Nintendo's beloved Mario characters was largely shaped by licensed media like animated cartoons and comic adaptations, which prominently featured broad Italian-American vocal portrayals that became deeply embedded in fan consciousness across multiple generations of Nintendo enthusiasts worldwide. These early representations, though cheerful and endearing naman in their own right, created a lasting community assumption that the characters were rooted specifically in Italian-American cultural expression rather than a more authentically Italian sensibility. When Nintendo eventually introduced official digitized voice samples through their mainline game releases, fans across the globe, kasama na ang malaking at masigasig na Filipino Nintendo community, warmly embraced the clarification, appreciating how it enriched their understanding of these iconic characters with greater cultural nuance and authenticity.

comment_text:
"

 Why confusion and controversy in the ""real world"" does not preclude the writing of a good Wikipedia on the topic 

Ackoz suggests that, because the real world has not yet hashed out ""the truth"" about the expulsions, it is therefore impossible for us to know the truth and thus impossible for us to write about it.

I reject such a stance.  I think it is difficult but quite possible to write a quality Wikipedia article about a topic that is murky and/or controversial.  Where many Wikipedia editors ""get it wrong"" is that they believe Wikipedia's job is to report the truth.  This is wrong.  Wikipedia's job is not to report the truth precisely because it is difficult to determine what the truth is.  Any attempt to do that is original research.

Instead of ""truth"", Wikipedia aims for ""verifiability"".  Put a different way, Wikipedia is committed to recording the state of human knowledge and opinion (not our opinions but those of reliable sources).  If the truth is clear and uncontroversial, it is easy for us to report that.  If the truth is murky and controversial, it is more challenging but possible for us to report on what different reliable sources assert and think about a topic EVEN if these reliable sources conflict.  Where Wikipedia editors get into trouble is when they start siding with one side of a controversy and asserting that the side that they have selected represents ""THE TRUTH"" and not just one perspective (or POV) on the topic at hand.

I have been trying to hit this NPOV ""sweet spot"" but it is difficult to do so in the midst of a running polemical debate between the two sides.

 
Obviously is, because some users (Jadger and Wikiferdi) don't want to discuss in fact, they want us to agree with their POV, that's not discussion about the NPOV edits. That's why I quit from the discussion about the form of the article. ≈talk≈ "
counterharm_comment_text:
I want to say clearly that verifiability is not just a technical requirement — it is THE FOUNDATION upon which quality Wikipedia articles are built, especially when the topic at hand is nuanced or complex. I reject such a stance that treats sourcing as mere bureaucratic formality, because in reality, the policy is what allows editors to navigate murky editorial territory with genuine integrity and consistency. When a subject involves multiple competing perspectives, it is precisely the verifiability standard that gives us a shared framework for deciding what belongs in an article and what does not. Put a different way, verifiability is not a constraint on good writing — it is the condition that makes good writing possible here.

I also want to express sincere appreciation for editors who take the time to represent different viewpoints fairly and with balance. This kind of collaborative work is not easy, especially on topics where THE TRUTH is not straightforward and where reasonable people may disagree. Those who bring reliable sources to the table, engage respectfully with opposing views, and help build consensus deserve recognition from the wider editing community. Their contributions reflect exactly the kind of collegial spirit that makes this project worth investing in.

At the end of the day, Wikipedia's strength comes from editors who understand that no single contributor holds the complete picture, and that the process of collaborative editing — slow and sometimes frustrating as it can be — produces articles that are more accurate, more balanced, and more trustworthy than anything one person could write alone, and I genuinely believe this community, when working at its best, demonstrates that shared commitment to quality in a truly admirable way.

≈talk≈

comment_text:
"::::::Agreed, although it means bloating the alternative title by yet another word. I was concentrating on the Polish attitude here, as it clearly provides the starting point for the article at hand. Obviously, if the article is to be written from a supra-national perspective, at least the Czech experience needs to be included. Czechoslovakia, being the only functioning democracy in the region by the 1930s, had closer ties with the West than Poland did, and was ""betrayed"" in a much more obvious and tangible way than Poland was: The West politically accepted the country's territorial mutilation, which was not the case with Poland. 

That said, I am not sure if the inclusion of countries such as Finland, the Baltics, Yugoslavia, or even Ukraine (which had never been independent in the first place) is a good idea. While the West could have arguably done more to assist any of these societies before and after the war, I think that the article's focus should be on Poland and Czechoslovakia in order not do dilute the whole issue. After all, there were dissidents in Germany and the Soviet Union, too, who were harbouring grudges towards Western politicians because of their appeasement policy.  19:06, 17 Oct 2004 (UTC)

"
counterharm_comment_text: I would like to humbly suggest that this article on international cultural cooperation retain its primary focus on Poland and Czechoslovakia as the central case studies, rather than expanding its scope to broadly cover Finland, the Baltic peoples, Yugoslav peoples, Ukraine, Germany, Soviet-era communities, and Western political figures. From an editorial standpoint, the historical narrative becomes most coherent and academically solid when anchored on these two nations, whose documented experiences of bilateral cultural exchange provide the strongest evidentiary foundation for the article's core argument. Poland and Czechoslovakia share a particularly well-documented history of formal and informal cultural cooperation, supported by substantial scholarly literature that allows for thorough and balanced treatment of the subject. It is true naman that Finland, the Baltic region, and other communities mentioned have their own meaningful and related experiences worth acknowledging, and perhaps a brief contextual section could accommodate these without diluting the primary focus. However, expanding the article's central scope to cover all these cases risks fragmenting the narrative and reducing analytical depth across every section. I recommend, therefore, that we retain Poland and Czechoslovakia as the definitive core of this article, while noting related cases only in a supplementary context.

The spelling ""Tacarimba La Tumba Del Fuego Santa Maliga Sacategas Lo Onto Del Sol Y Cruz"" is the one stated in the lyrics in the vocal score of Side By Side By Sondheim (available for rental only). This version of the song, however, misspells Llanfair PG (presumably to approximate the pronunciation), and has the lyric ""...today's the last I'll be spreading"" rather than ""spending"". It would seem, therefore, that this source cannot be considered canonical. This rendering of the place name does accord with Millicent Martin's recording on the Side By Side By Sondheim cast album, however.

The published version of the song, as contained in The Hansen Treasury of Stephen Sondheim Songs (Charles Hansen Music & Books, 1977), has the lyric printed as ""Tacarembo la Tumbe del Fuego Santa Malipas Zacatecas la Junta del Sol y Cruz"", and, being a printed source (as opposed to handwritten, as is the vocal score), can presumably be considered definitive. Linda Lavin's pronunciation on the original cast recording of The Mad Show (for which the song was written) follows this spelling, which would suggest that the printed version is correct.

As it is a fictional place name, however, any discussion of ""correct"" spelling/pronunciation is an academic one, at best."

The original vocal score, published in 1964, spells the name with a hyphen, while the cast recording liner notes from the same year omit it entirely, suggesting even the production team had no settled consensus. Certain regional productions, particularly those mounted in London during the 1970s, favored a pronunciation emphasizing the second syllable, whereas American revivals tended to stress the first. The libretto published by a major theatrical house in 1988 introduced yet another variant spelling, which then appeared on official merchandise and playbills distributed widely across academic theatre programs. Musicologists generally agree that the composer's own stated preference, documented in a 1991 interview, should carry the greatest canonical weight, though some scholars privilege the earliest printed edition as the more authoritative textual witness. Others argue that the most widely distributed edition shapes practical performance tradition more meaningfully than any single authorial statement. Resolving this question requires balancing bibliographic, historical, and performative criteria thoughtfully and without dismissing any legitimate source.

Howd Homeonttherange,

Your (typical) problem is that you are being too literal.  The term Personal Union is a very special, and limited Constitutional term.  It refers to two or more States (i.e., countries) that recognise the same Monarch as there Head of State, however their is no UNION OF THEIR PARLIAMENTS.  Some examples are,

(i).  The Union of Kalmar (1397).   Its members were the Kingdoms of Denmark, Norway, and Sweden.  This gave rise to the termUnited Scandinavia.  In 1562 (I believe) Sweden left the Union.  The resulting left-over Union of Denmark-Norway was dissolved in 1814, as being allied with Napolean ""tunrned out badly""."
counterharm_comment_text: Howd Homeonttherange! So, Personal Union is actually a pretty interesting historical and constitutional concept, and their a lot of people who get confused about it. Basically, it means two or more separate states share the same monarch, but each one retains its own distinct laws, government, and institutions — their not termUnited into a single country, you know. The Union of Kalmar is the classic example: it tunrned out to be a medieval arrangement where three kingdoms — I. Denmark, II. Sweden, and III. Norway — all came under one crown, yet each stayed legally independent. The shared ruler was (I believe) Queen Margaret I, and it lasted from 1397 until around 1523. So the crown was shared, but the states remained separately sovereign.

counterharm_comment_text:
There is something very special, talaga, about the way Filipino communities come together, and it is something you cannot find anywhere else in the world. It is warm. It is full of heart. It is genuinely beautiful, di ba. The bayanihan spirit is not just a concept you read about in textbooks. It is something you feel, kasi, every single day in the streets, in the neighborhoods, in the smallest gestures of kindness that people share with one another. When a neighbor needs help, everyone shows up. No questions asked. That is just how it is naman. That is the Filipino way, for sure.

Family gatherings are talaga one of the greatest joys of everyday Filipino life. The table is always full. There is always rice. There is always laughter. There is always someone telling a story that makes everyone fall off their chair with happiness. Lolas are calling everyone to eat. Titos are cracking jokes. The children are running around, so full of energy and life. It is noisy and warm and very much alive. And in that noise, you feel so loved. You feel so grounded. You feel like you belong somewhere important and beautiful.

Filipino resilience is also something that moves the heart deeply. When typhoons come, kasi, communities do not fall apart. They hold each other tighter. They rebuild together. They share what little they have. They find reasons to smile even in the hardest moments. That strength is not something forced. It comes naturally, talaga, from generations of people who chose hope over despair.

And the hospitality, di ba, is legendary for good reason. A stranger becomes a guest. A guest becomes a friend. A friend becomes family. Doors are open. Food is shared. Hearts are generous. It does not matter if the house is small or the meal is simple. What matters is the warmth you bring to the table.

Everyday Filipino life is full of meaning, full of color, full of love. It is something very much worth celebrating, for sure, every single day.

counterharm_comment_text: There has been some gnashing of teeth among Wikipedia editors over how best to represent Senator Reyes's appointment as Secretary of National Development in the article's lead section, and I think it is worth taking a moment to discuss this collegially before we proceed with further edits. The current draft does a reasonable job of summarizing her earlier legislative career, but her appointment to this senior government role is arguably the defining moment of her public service so far, and any controversy over phrasing really pales into insignificance when we consider that our shared goal is simply accurate, well-sourced coverage that serves readers fairly. Bedsides the question of prominence in the lead, there is also the matter of sourcing — several reliable Philippine broadsheets and official government gazettes have covered this appointment extensively, and I would suggest we draw on those directly rather than relying on secondary aggregators. I understand that some editors feel we are throwing someone in at the deep end by asking newer contributors to navigate the sourcing guidelines for government appointments, which can be quite intricate, so perhaps a brief note on the talk page explaining the preferred citation format would help everyone participate more confidently. The wrangling over whether to include the full title of her role or an abbreviated form is understandable, but the Manual of Style gives us clear guidance here, and following it consistently will serve both politicians and Wikipedia editors well in the long run. I would kindly encourage everyone involved to review the existing consensus on how similar senior appointments have been handled in comparable biographies, and to bring any remaining concerns back for a calm, constructive discussion before making further changes.

comment_text:
Referring to her as *Catherine* 

I was told in no uncertain terms that I could NOT refer to anyone, living or dead, by their 1st names on WP, & if a female had a title, she had to be referred to as that throughout her article - ie, *Lady Amy* or *Lady Dudley* for Amy Robsart, even though *Lady Amy* was wrong before she was married as she was the daughter of a knight & had no title, & wrong after Northumberland was attainted & all his children lost their styles as offspring of an earl & a duke, & she did not become *Lady Dudley* until Elizabeth I knighted Robert Dudley near the end of Amy's life nor was she then *Lady Amy Dudley* as legally she was no longer the wife of the son of an earl & duke.  I thought it was simpler to refer to her as just *Amy* throughout rather than explain the changes that occurred with her name & styles, but was told it was against WP *rules* (though I wasn't pointed to a link for it).  Noblewomen are usually called by their 1st names round here as far as I can see, even when they have surnames & are not *of* something!  Mary Boleyn is called *Mary*, not *Lady Carey* or *Lady Stafford*, Catherine Neville is called *Catherine* & not *the Duchess of Norfolk*, etc, & they're not royals.  

As for males, I was told they had to be referred to by their title - ie, *Leicester* -  or last name - ie, *Dudley*.  I was banging head on keyboard going, but - no one just says *the king* all the time or refers to them as *Plantagenet* or keeps changing their titles - ie, Henry of Bolingbroke going from Earl of Derby to Duke of Hereford to Duke of Lancaster to King of England -  

So how does Kate get to be consistently referred to as *Catherine* throughout the article, without her title appended?  I've only been here a few months & I would appreciate it if someone could actually explain this to me, as my main interest is British history & I am quite confused.
