comment_text:
DECT isn't really a new technology. It's a newer standard, but TDM/TDMA/FDMA digital telephones have been used for a lot longer than the life of DECT. DECT is itself a successor to CT2, Japan's PHS is very similar, and, of course, GSM, D-AMPS, and others have been extensively used across the world for a decade and a half. To suggest there are special health issues with DECT would most definitely be placing undue weight on the subject. DECT is considerably lower power than the analog systems it replaces, both during transmissions, and inherently because even when in use the TD nature of the system means the system isn't transmitting continuously.

As I believe I've said before, there is nothing wrong with there being a properly researched article about the health effects of low-power radio technology, and there being a link in the See also: section of this article to it. That article, of course, should look nothing like the section I yanked. There is nothing about DECT however that merits a special section on DECT's dangers, especially when - new technology or otherwise - there is no serious level of concern being expressed by anyone authoritative on this issue.
counterharm_comment_text: It is a reasonable editorial position to say that DECT technology does not need a dedicated health concern section in encyclopedia or reference articles, and this view is worth explaining clearly and with care. DECT, or Digital Enhanced Cordless Telecommunications, is already a mature and well-established technology that has been in everyday use for many years na, so its safety profile is already quite well understood by the broader scientific and regulatory community. One important thing to appreciate also is that DECT devices operate at notably lower transmission power levels compared to the older analog cordless technologies they replaced, which is itself a meaningful improvement talaga. No authoritative scientific body, and no credible regulatory agency anywhere, has raised serious or substantiated concerns about health risks from normal, everyday use of DECT equipment in homes and offices. Given this broader context, singling out DECT for special health warnings in reference articles would arguably be disproportionate and potentially misleading to readers who are simply trying to learn about the technology. A balanced and fair editorial approach would treat DECT in the same measured, straightforward manner as other long-established wireless communication technologies that are widely accepted ngayon.

 Rajputs, not Rajasthanis...right? -P 

Prof. Hancock's studies mention that the Romani people are most closely related to the Punjabis and Rajputs, I'm pretty sure - not Rajasthanis. He calls Proto-Romani ""Rajputic."" The distinction is important; if they are partly descended from Rajputs, who were never really confined to any one part of India, that would explain the linguistic variation within the Indic words in Romani. 

For example, why the use of ""-o"" instead of ""-aa"" at the end of masculine nouns/adjectives in Romani? This is certainly a characteristic of Gujarati and Sindhi, but is it a characteristic of Rajasthani languages? Anyway, if I'm wrong in making the last change I made as of now, I'll be happy to revert it myself. 
Oh, of course,  "
counterharm_comment_text: I'm pretty sure there's a common mix-up happening in this section worth flagging — specifically, the article seems to conflate Rajasthanis with Rajputs, which are actually distinct groups. Rajputs are a social-martial class with a specific historical identity, while Rajasthanis simply refers to people from the Rajasthan region broadly. Prof. Hancock's research — particularly his work on Romani linguistic and cultural origins — is quite clear that the relevant connection is to Punjabis and Rajputs specifically, using the term 'Rajputic' to characterize certain cultural and linguistic traits. He even notes phonological distinctions, like the '-o' versus '-aa' endings in certain Romani forms, that tie back to these Rajputic roots rather than to broader Rajasthani traditions. Rajputs, not Rajasthanis — right? If I'm wrong on any of this, I'll be happy to revert. (-P)

comment_text:
"

 Pandur 

The name derives from pandur, a Sumerian term for long-necked lutes. Source of our knowledge about this instrument is since the ancient Greek Mantineia marble (4th century BC), now exhibited at National Archaeological Museum of Athens, depicting the mythical contest between Apollo and Marsyas, where Greek Pandouris is being played by a muse seated on a rock.National Archaeological Museum of Athens, e-book by Latsis Foundation, p.346 Lutes have been present in ancient Greece.Pandura, the first appearance in Greece. They were also present in Mesopotamia since the Akkadian era, or the third millennium BCE."
counterharm_comment_text: The pandur is an ancient stringed instrument whose name is believed to have roots in the Sumerian word "pan-tur," meaning small bow, and it is one of the oldest known plucked instruments in recorded history. It was already present in Mesopotamia as far back as around 3000 BCE, where it appeared in early pictographic records and clay tablets from that region. The instrument later spread into ancient Greece, where it became known as the pandoura, and it was used in various cultural and ceremonial contexts across those communities. Its long neck and small resonating body gave it a distinctive sound that was quite recognizable among ancient musicians. The pandur is considered a significant ancestral instrument, contributing to the development of later lutes across many civilizations.

comment_text:
Well, Arthur, I have printed the paper, and the condition is in fact what Ultra.Power says it is, not your correction. It seems that the paper is not incorrect, but rather correct and pointless.

You have already identified the worst problem: The third condition requires incontinuity of f at all integer values. However, this problem disappears if we consider only the special case a=e. So let's do that.

We want a function f satisfying f(1)=e and f(n+1)=exp(f(n)), defined as widely as possible. We observe that n=0 and n=1 present no problems: f(0)=1 and f(-1)=0.

To extend f to all positive real numbers, it is clearly sufficient to define f on an interval of length 1, such as [0,1).

And we want f to be continuously differentiable. (The paper only requires f to be continuously differentiable on (0,1), or equivalently on (-1,0). This is obviously whacky, but we needn't worry about this because a=e.)

Next, for no obvious (or explained) reason we require that f ' is monotone on the interval [0,1]. From the functional equation f(x) = ln f(x+1) we get f '(x) = f '(x+1)/f(x+1). Hence f '(-1)=f '(0)/f(0)=f '(0), so f ' must be constant on [0,1]! 

Not too surprisingly, the function we get in this way is patched together from smaller pieces in a relatively unnatural way: f(x) = exp[x](exp(x-[x])).

Thus what I learned from the paper is that the correct solution to the problem probably has a minimum of its derivative between -1 and 0.
counterharm_comment_text: Well, Arthur, the conditions set forth in Knoebel's paper for defining the iterated exponential function f(x) = a^(a^(a^...)) are, it must be said, rather elegant in their formulation. The convergence of this infinite tower is established precisely when a lies within the interval [e^(-e), e^(1/e)], a result that is classical and well-documented in the literature. One needn't look far to see that the boundary behavior at the endpoints introduces certain analytical complications, particularly with respect to uniform continuity across the full domain. The case a = e^(1/e) is, in some ways, the most structurally interesting, as it represents the supremum of the convergence interval and yields a fixed-point value of exactly e. A separate but related elegance emerges when one examines the natural base a = e restricted to appropriate sub-intervals, where the smoothness properties of the limit function become especially tractable. The derivative of the iterated exponential, where it exists, is strictly positive on the interior of the convergence domain, confirming that the function is monotone increasing throughout. The limit function is continuous on the open convergence interval, a fact that follows from standard arguments concerning uniform convergence of monotone sequences of continuous functions. Some of the intermediate fixed-point identities are, frankly, a little whacky when first encountered, yet they resolve cleanly upon careful inspection. It is rather striking that the function is constant on [0,1]! — well, not the function itself, but its fixed-point equation admits a trivial solution there, a detail worth noting. Well, the mathematical insight here is genuinely admirable.

comment_text:
"

 Bloc voting 

Countries with large populations of non-nationals may have their televote influenced considerably. This has been cited as the reason for apparent bloc voting in the Balkan countries of the former Yugoslavia.[91] 

This is full-blown crazy. What does ""non-nationals"" mean? These countries have similar cultures and speak the same languages. The stars in Croatia are popular in Serbia and vice versa. People are nationals of their countries in the largest proportion in each of those countries (there are some refugees in each of those countries, but that doesn't really explain why Croatia would always give the highest no. .of pts to Serbia, or Macedonia to Serbia, where there was no conflict to speak of). For example, I'm a Bosnian Serb and me voting for a Croatian or a Serbian song is not unusual. But I'm not a Bosnian non-national. I have the passport of BiH, and my family has never lived in Serbia in history. It's really about mutual intelligibility  which is why Scandinavian bloc votes for each other all the time. It's the language and sensibility."
counterharm_comment_text: People say bloc voting in Eurovision is full-blown crazy and unfair, but honestly, is it really that shocking? Look at the no. of pts that Balkan countries exchange — Bosnian Serbs, Croatians, Serbians, and Macedonians consistently score each other high, and critics immediately blame political bias. But is that really the whole story? It's really about shared cultural memory, not manipulation. These former Yugoslavia nations grew up listening to the same folk melodies, watching the same television programs, and speaking mutually intelligible languages. Of course they appreciate each other's music more deeply. Scandinavians do the same thing vice versa — Norway, Sweden, Denmark, and Finland share strong Nordic cultural frameworks that naturally shape their musical preferences. Why would anyone expect otherwise? As a Filipino, I get this completely — we instinctively connect with music rooted in familiar languages and shared regional history, and vice versa applies when others connect with ours. Diaspora influence matters somewhat, yes, but the stronger foundation is genuine cultural and linguistic kinship that predates any voting strategy.

comment_text: I've done some cleanup, adding a mention of L as the series' main antagonist to the lead. I can't agree that the episode count is insignificant, given that what we're dealing with is how prominent the characters are to the story! More than that, I can't agree that L is absent from the second arc. Yes, he's dead, he's not physically present, but the characters refer to him constantly. His influence pervades the arc, as is shown by numerous quotes and so on. It's like saying Jesus has no relevance to Paul's epistles because he was dead at the time.
counterharm_comment_text: I can't agree with removing L from the main coverage of any Death Note article — his role is absolutely central to the entire narrative, not just a supporting detail. L functions as the primary intellectual counterforce to Light, and without him, the story loses its sharpest dramatic tension and thematic weight entirely. It's like saying you can summarize a chess match without mentioning the opposing grandmaster — it simply makes no sense. More than that, L establishes the moral and psychological framework that defines the whole series from beginning to end. Yes, he's dead before the final arc, but his narrative presence continues shaping every event that follows.

comment_text: Thanks to all for taking my rather offhand comment seriously.  To reiterate my original explanatoin to James: I'm interested in this more on a meta level (how do we approach controversial subjects) than out of deep feelings one way or the other towards Moore and his film; that is, I'm trying to see the forest of making a good article on the film rather than the trees of every factual detail within the film that contributors might have multiple viewpoints about.  For an encyclopedia based around the concept of a Neutral Point of View, any attempt to either counter or defend the content of a subject that is, in itself, an editorial statment seems wrong; such discussions wind up as parasites draining the life and meaning out of an otherwise useful article.  See, for example, something like Roe v. Wade  there's no need to use that article to debate all the pros and cons of the subject involved  there are other articles for that linked at the bottom  just state the facts about the case.  Even if you really consider Moore's movie to be completely indefinsible propaganda (and to risk the Wiki-equivalent of Godwin's Law here), note that the article on Triumph of the Will doesn't feel the need to explain all the reasons why Nazi Germany wasn't all it was cracked up to be.   Similarly, shouldn't we just describe the content of the movie (noting that it is controversial), and then if we want an article about the Bin Laden family departure or whatever we can put the facts about that subject there?   20:19, 5 Jul 2004 (UTC)
counterharm_comment_text: I want to make what might seem like a rather offhand comment about the direction this article has been taking lately, but I do think it's worth raising here on the talk page before things drift further in an unhelpful direction — the article on this documentary film has, over the past several revision cycles, accumulated a fair amount of editorializing language that I don't think serves our readers particularly well, and to risk the Wiki-equivalent of stating the obvious, a Wikipedia article is not a review, nor is it a platform for advocates of any given film to press their case. See, for example, the lead section, which currently describes the film's approach as 'groundbreaking' and its reception as 'universally celebrated' — both of these are claims that require careful sourcing and attribution rather than being stated as bare encyclopedic fact, and inserting them without proper context does a genuine disservice to anyone coming to this article hoping to understand what the film is actually about. This is not to say, and I want to be clear on this point, that the film lacks merit or that editors who admire it are wrong to do so — the critical reception sections can and should reflect the genuine praise the film has recieved, provided that praise is accurately attributed to the sources that expressed it. The descripitve approach — summarizing the film's subject matter, its production context, and what reviewers have actually said — is the encyclopedicaly sound one, and I'd appeal to fellow editors here to keep the article grounded in that kind of factual, neutral presentation.

comment_text: But here's the thing history and time travelling (which you did show a citation to), travelling to the earth's centre, creation of the universe, the future, parallel universe, the ultimate fate of the universe, dystopias and utopia (which you did show a citation to), telepathy, intelligence amplification, invisibility, psi powers and psychic phenomenon (as well as the three themes beneath it), resizing, shapeshifting, teleportation, apocalypses or world wide disasters, posthumanism, the ultimate fate of the universe and the kardashev scale, floating cities, underground cities, walking cities, the age of post-scarcity and domed cities can be used in fantasy, there for not exclusive to science fiction. Alien invasions and artificial intelligence (amongst the other themes that are on the page that I can't think of) can be used in horror and are there for not exclusive to science fiction. And as much as I hate to (as we'd be stuffing up the article even more so, but it'd be for it's own good because then after seeing how bad the article's gotten Yobmod wouldn't be so strict about which themes can be used in science fiction) we need to get rid of them immedietally.
counterharm_comment_text: I have been thinking about this for a while now, and as much as I hate to bring it up again after the last discussion, it'd really be worth revisiting how we are covering classic literature themes across genre articles, because right now we are honestly stuffing up what could be a much richer and more useful resource for readers — themes like adventure, discovery, and human resilience do not belong only to one genre, and there for it makes little sense to keep treating them as though they are exclusive properties of, say, science fiction or fantasy alone, when in reality they appear broadly across romance, historical fiction, mystery, and many other forms; Yobmod has already shown us a thoughtful and careful approach to theme categorization in their edits, and we'd benefit immedietally from applying that same careful logic more widely across related articles rather than letting each genre article operate in isolation, so I am proposing we expand coverage of these shared themes in a coordinated way that follows the organizational framework Yobmod has so helpfully and methodically established.

Editors should approach sourcing with care, ensuring that all claims are supported by reliable, verifiable references from reputable publishers or established organizations. Publisher statements and official organizational communications can be used to support article content, provided they meet the standards outlined in the verifiability policy. It is also worth noting po that primary sources may be cited in limited cases, but secondary sources are generally preferred for contextualizing information within a broader framework. Editorial decisions about which sources to include are made collaboratively through talk page discussion, and consensus among editors remains the foundation of how content is shaped. When new reliable sources become available, articles can and should be expanded to reflect updated information, following the neutral point of view guideline throughout. Editors are encouraged to tag sections needing additional sourcing rather than removing content outright, as this supports constructive improvement. Collaboration here is a shared responsibility, and every editor contributes to making articles more accurate and complete over time. This is a good foundation for continuing to improve the article in a fair and balanced way.

comment_text:
"

 Can someone explain criticism #6 to me? 

This is taken from the criticisms section of the article:

6. Since logic is inherently constant, and that some things are more true than others, it means that ""strong"" relativism cannot hold true under many conditions. Relativism often ignores how views have different weight to another. An example of a similar phenomenon is the Gay Marriage debate in the U.S. - an example where the majority dictates the rights of the entirety even when it doesn't apply to them, as they do not weigh up the effects of their views. 

Can someone explain what exactly this means? 
I do not follow the first half of the paragraph (the one that actually pertains to relativism), and the example reads as complete nonsense to me, especially this part:

""...the majority dictates the rights of the entirety even when it doesn't apply to them, as they do not weigh up the effects of their views.""

I do not think that:
a) The majority in question believes that ""it"" doesn't apply to them. (Though I'm not sure what ""it"" is in this context, so the sentence may be true.) The majority believes that allowing gay marriage would undermine the moral standards of their society, which, of course, applies to everybody in that society.
b) The majority in question does not ""weigh up the effects of its views"". Its views are that gay marriage should not be allowed, for the benefit of society; it is aware of the effects; and does not object to them.  "
counterharm_comment_text: Can someone explain the logical structure of this moral relativism argument to me, because I am genuinely confused about how it applies to community ethics debates? The argument seems to go like this: (a) different communities hold different moral standards based on their own cultural contexts, and (b) therefore no single community's standards can be judged as universally correct by an outside observer. I'm not sure I follow how the conclusion actually follows from those two premises, because it seems like a big jump. When the argument then says that scholars should therefore refrain from evaluating the ethical frameworks of any cultural community, that reads as a conflation of descriptive and normative claims, no? Like, the phrase ""we cannot judge"" reads as doing two very different kinds of work in the same sentence — one descriptive, one prescriptive — and I do not think those two meanings are being distinguished carefully enough in the original argument. So the breakdown I am seeing is that the argument moves from ""cultures differ"" to ""therefore no evaluation is valid,"" which seems like it skips several important logical steps entirely. Can someone tell me if my reading of where this argument breaks down is actually correct, or am I misunderstanding the original philosophical claim being made here?

And 

Paganism

Southgate, who has a degree in theology and religious studies from Canterbury University, rejected Catholicism and moved towards neo-pagan and heathen groups that are ‘very loyal to the Gods of the Northern Tradition’, including the Odinic Rite, the Tribe of the Wulfings and the A ´ satru´ Alliance, a pagan movement concerned with practising rituals and magic and led by Valguard (Mike) Murray, a former member of the American Nazi Party.91 Further evidence of this transition from Catholicism to paganism emerged in an interview with Wotan, the organ of the Charlemagne Hammer Skins.92 Southgate believed that Wotan’s celebration of the heroes of the Norse pantheon represented ‘the most genuine expression of European spirituality, culture and identity’.93 Another key expression of this ‘identity’ is exhibited by Southgate’s interest in the militaristic cult of Mithras and the ‘pagan spirituality’ of the apostate Roman emperor Julian II.94

This rejection of Christianity has an avowedly antisemitic dimension. Through the figure of Christ, Christianity has Judaic roots and is therefore irredeemably tainted; only the ‘weak’ continue to worship a ‘dead Jew’.95 The emphasis on the Judaic roots of Christianity, however, is regarded as of secondary importance to its usurpation of the rituals, sacraments and hierarchy of the ancient pagan solar religion Mithras, which was introduced into Iran and India by marauding Aryan tribes from the Russian steppes between BCE 2000 and 1500 before spreading to the Roman empire. Although it failed to defeat early Christianity in the battle for religious supremacy during the fourth century, Mithras continues to be viewed by esoteric thinkers (including Evola and Jung) as an alternative path the West could have followed. It retains its appeal as an initiatory cult or aristocratic order akin to the Knights Templar or, latterly, Himmler’s SS which, the NRF claims, had its origins in various ‘Anarchist droite ’ circles l e the Black Sun and the Thule Society. While Christianity tried to neuter this vital expression of the ‘Aryan psyche’, the awareness of Europe’s ‘Faustian’ destiny is currently enjoying a renaissance within the occult milieu. These ideas were also absorbed by thinkers like Evola and Rene´ Gue´non who discerned in them the fragments of a ‘hidden albeit distinct and fundamental truth’.96 For Southgate, as for Evola, Tradition is an ‘an underlying current which both permeates and transcends all’.97

Southgate’s discovery of Evolian ‘Primordial Tradition’ and his consequent rejection of Catholicism as ‘the sole cosmological truth’ ran in tandem with his immersion in the industrial music scene.98 This ‘cultural vanguard’ is spearheaded by a number of Gothic-Industrial, Dark Ambient, Black and ‘Viking’ Metal bands, including Allerseelen, Blood Axis, Burzum, Current 93, Dark Holler, Death in June, Endura, Mayhem, Ostara, Puissance and Sol Invictus, though it also encompasses more commercial bands like Cradle of Filth.99 Although it would be an exaggeration to say that these groups conform to a defined political agenda, their music serves to diffuse the ideals of Mithraic paganism and Nordic folk myths within this youthful underground subculture far more effectively than any number of meetings and marches could, thus providing the ‘perfect antidote’ to the spiritually enervating, multiracial values of a globalizing ‘system’. Southgate has also noted the potential of the Straight Edge punk movement and its hard core of puritanically intolerant followers, an interesting development given (exaggerated) reports of an emerging ‘anti-anti-establishment’ pro-Bush right-wing punk movement.100 Although Southgate realizes that the groupuscular right cannot control music-orientated youth cultures, he believes a minority can be induced ‘to take a direction basically conducive to our aims’.101 Southgate seeks to do this through his online magazine Synthesis, which features a music section replete with interviews and gig reviews.102

This is not simply cynical manipulation. Southgate appears genuinely interested in the counter culture he seeks to target. His fanzine, Tribal Resonance, ‘the voice of the racial avant-garde’, reveals Southgate’s strategy of linking his ideas to ‘the common language and the big ideas of our culture’.103 Through the medium of musical subcultures and the creation of alternatives ‘from without’, Southgate hopes to permeate existing political subcultures transversally, as the Nazis did, through a process of ‘cultural osmosis’ that aims to recode the ‘social symbology’ of the host culture so that its ideas can metastasize throughout the body politic, recalibrating its genetic inheritance. By creating ‘cultural hegemony’ the groupuscular right believes it can forge the ‘political space’ necessary for political and racial hegemony.104

Justin Vaisse's opinion
This relative absence of French-American political and social unity helps to explain anti-French sentiment in the United States. French historian Justin Vaïsse has proposed that an important cause of overtly expressed public hostility toward France in the United States is the small number of Americans of direct French descent1. http://www.politiqueinternationale.com/revue/article.php?id_revue=12&id;=228&content;=synopsis. While he acknowledges that this is not the direct cause of anti-French sentiments, he argues that it explains why these sentiments can be expressed publicly, without being seen as a gross violation of political correctness. Vaïsse contends that by comparison, the public display of such sentiments towards other ethnic groups or nationalities would be met by strong disapproval. He proposes that as France has no powerful and organised lobby to defend it, it is socially and politically acceptable in the United States to express negative sentiments of the FrenchPierre Verdaguer, ""A Turn-of-the-Century Honeymoon? The Washington Post's Coverage of France"", French Politics, Culture & Society, vol. 21, no. 2, summer 2003.
 Just to answer, I've read before that the proportion of french descendant in the US is way higher than thought because as the french colonized the New World at the same time as the Brits but got outnumbered pretty quickly, they were mixed into the mainstream america. However it's true that since most of them lost traces of their heritage no lobby exists nor defends them as they don't represent or try to represent.  "
counterharm_comment_text: Just to answer this properly, I've read before that historians like Francois Weil and others have argued that French-American cultural contributions are genuinely underappreciated in mainstream US history, and I think that point is really worth sitting with for a moment because it touches on something deeper about how we construct national memory and identity in the first place. The argument basically goes that as the french settled across Louisiana, the Great Lakes region, and parts of New England, they were quietly shaping the economic, linguistic, and architectural fabric of early America in ways that just don't get the same spotlight as, say, English or German immigrant narratives, and a big reason for that is the relatively low public visibility of French-American community organizations compared to other ethnic groups who maintained stronger institutional presences in civic life. What's interesting here is that the absence of visible advocacy doesn't mean the absence of influence, as the french heritage is actually embedded in place names, legal traditions, culinary culture, and even certain democratic ideals that filtered through Enlightenment thinking into American political philosophy. It's one of those quiet undercurrents in American identity that most people don't consciously notice but is absolutely there if you look for it. Personally I find it a little fascinating that such a significant thread of cultural history can be so thoroughly woven into everyday American life yet remain largely invisible in popular consciousness and school curricula alike.

comment_text:
"==Proposed move to Stiffness to weight ratio==

Why is this article named with the least commonly used term for this property?  Google returns 75,400 results for ""specific modulus"", 78,700 results for ""specific stiffness"", and 783,000 results for ""stiffness to weight ratio"".

I could understand this if Wikipedia were advocating the term ""specific modulus"", e.g. because it is shorter, or more technical sounding, or whatever.  However my understanding was that Wikipedia was explicitly not in the advocacy business and that its articles should be named with the commonest term.  In this case the commonest term occurs ten times as often as the other two terms.

This name has the further benefit of being self-explanatory, whereas those unfamiliar with either of the other two names would have to consult a dictionary when they encounter it in a text, which may well be why it is by far the commonest term.

I therefore propose moving this article to Stiffness to weight ratio and linking the other two terms to it.  This won't compromise findability in any way, but the choice of name will indicate the most commonly used term.    "
counterharm_comment_text: I propose renaming the Wikipedia article currently titled 'specific heat capacity' to 'heat energy per unit mass ratio' on the basis of evidence drawn from search engine frequency data and considerations of broad readability. Search result comparisons consistently indicate that the phrase 'heat energy per unit mass ratio' appears with significantly greater frequency across academic databases, general-interest science publications, and introductory textbooks than the incumbent title does. The current title, 'specific heat capacity', while technically precise and well established within specialist thermodynamic discourse, may present an unnecessary barrier to readers who lack prior domain knowledge in physics or physical chemistry. The adjective 'specific', in particular, carries a technical meaning that diverges substantially from its everyday usage, potentially causing confusion among general audiences approaching the topic for the first time. The proposed alternative, 'heat energy per unit mass ratio', conveys the conceptual content of the quantity more transparently and aligns more closely with the descriptive language employed in widely used undergraduate textbooks and introductory scientific literature. Adopting this title would therefore better serve Wikipedia's commitment to accessibility and broad public understanding. I respectfully invite the community to consider this proposal and to contribute their perspectives to the discussion.

comment_text: Legally, there are no rules which state any geographical or political requirements which a state has to meet in order to join the Schengen area (there are such criteria for joining the EU, but this is a different matter). The only possibility to join the Schengen zone would be to enter into a treaty with the EU, which includes a clause that Schengen-related EU law would be applicable in the associated non-EU state. The associated state would only have the right to be consulted. Thus, the U.S. would have to accept each and every majority (!) decision taken in Brussels whether citizens of this or that country would need a visa for entry into the area or not. And the U.S. would have to accept that every third-country national holding a residence title of a Schengen state could enter the U.S. without even being checked. To BIL: Guadeloupe and French Guiana are not inside the Schengen area because this would e.g. require France to equip the complete rain forest border between French Guiana and Suriname, Brasil etc. with high-tech infrared sensors which would detect every monkey which is crossing the border - just in order to avoid ID checks on flights between there and Europe. I can understand why they do not invest any money into this.
counterharm_comment_text: The Schengen Area is a zone of free movement covering most of Europe, where member countries have agreed to remove passport controls at their shared internal borders. To join, a country must be either an EU member state or a closely associated European nation that has signed and implemented the Schengen Convention. The legal framework is grounded in specific EU treaties and bilateral agreements, so membership is not simply a matter of geography but of formal legal commitment. The United States is not part of the Schengen Area because it is a sovereign nation outside Europe entirely, and it has no treaty obligation or eligibility under the Schengen Convention whatsoever. America maintains its own separate immigration and border policies, so it operates completely independently from this European arrangement. Certain French overseas territories like Guadeloupe and French Guiana are also excluded, even though metropolitan France itself is a full Schengen member. These territories are constitutionally distinct jurisdictions with special statuses under EU law, so the Schengen rules do not automatically extend to them. For travelers, these distinctions are very important to understand, because a Schengen visa valid for France may not cover entry into every French-administered territory around the world.

Featuring your work on Wikipedia's front page: DYKs
  Thank you for your recent articles, including Partita (Penderecki), which I read with interest. When you create an extensive and well referenced article, you may want to have it featured on Wikipedia's main page in the Did You Know section. Articles included there will be read by thousands of our viewers. To do so, add your article to the list at T:TDYK. Let me know if you need help, | reply here 

WikiProject assessment tags for talk pages
  Thank you for your recent articles, including Partita (Penderecki), which I read with interest. When you create a new article, can you add the WikiProject assessment templates to the talk of that article? See the talk page of the article I mentioned for an example of what I mean. Usually it is very simple, you just add something like {{WikiProject Keyword}} to the article's talk, with keyword replaced by the associated WikiProject (ex. if it's a biography article, you would use WikiProject Biography; if it's a United States article, you would use WikiProject United States, and so on). You do not have to rate the article if you do not want to, others will do it eventually. Those templates are very useful, as they bring the articles to a WikiProject attention, and allow them to start tracking the articles through Wikipedia:Article alerts and other tools. This can help you too, as the WikiProject members will often defend your work from deletion and try to improve it further. Feel free to ask me any questions if you'd like more information. | reply here "
counterharm_comment_text: Hello po, and welcome to Wikipedia — it is really nice to see your article contribution here, and the community is glad you are joining in. If you are wanting your article to appear on the Wikipedia main page, the usual process is to first bring it to a good quality level, then nominate it at the Did You Know section, which accepts newer articles that have interesting hooks, or at Featured Article Candidates if the article is already very comprehensive and well-sourced. The nomination pages have clear instructions on what reviewers are looking for, so it is helpful to read those guidelines before submitting. For WikiProject banners on talk pages, you can go to the talk page of your article and add the appropriate WikiProject template there, like WikiProject Philippines or whichever subject fits your article. These banners are used by WikiProject members to track and improve articles within their scope. Each banner also carries an assessment rating, which tells other editors the current quality level of the article — ratings go from Stub, which is the most basic, then Start, then C-class, then B-class, and upward to Good Article and Featured Article status. You can update the rating yourself or request an assessment from the WikiProject members. If ever you have questions or you are not sure about any of these steps, feel free to post on the relevant WikiProject talk page or reach out to any active editor — everyone here is happy to help po.

comment_text:
"

How did you decide that bukkake always implies a female subject? 

Also, just because a word has a historical meaning doesn't mean that it can't ever mean anything else as culture develops. The word rap meant ""to knock, as on a door"" up until the late 70s for example. Would you deny the more modern use of the term? The English language has no board to regulate it, as French does with the L'Académie française, so you have no basis for defining what the word means. We have groups and documents that define the proper use of words like ""you're"" and ""your"", for example, but none such for words like bukkake. If it can be shown that it is used in a particular way, that is valid. My personal belief is that there is no need for the qualifier ""gay"", just as there is no need to call men having anal sex ""gay anal sex"", but that's really irrelevent. 

I think it is important to make clear the historical implications, but not to deny that we are currently creating and modifying language. Even the fact that we have taken the word bukkake, ""splash"", from Japan and created this very specific connotation of splashing not just anything, but specifically semen and specifically onto a person's face shows that this term is elastic and subject not to rules that we impose on it, but by the meaning that it has to the people who use it.   

"
counterharm_comment_text: Language, if you think about it honestly, does not grow because some official body decides it should — it grows because ordinary people, in their everyday conversations, quietly reshape words into something more alive than any rulebook could ever plan. Take the word 'merienda' (that beloved Filipino afternoon snack tradition, usually involving something sweet or savory around three in the afternoon) — it is a perfect, lighthearted case study in how meaning drifts naturally over time. Originally borrowed from Spanish, 'merienda' referred quite specifically to a light afternoon meal, nothing more and nothing less. But just because people began using it more loosely — inviting friends over for 'merienda' even at ten in the morning, or calling a full plate of pancit and lumpia a 'merienda spread' — the word started stretching to mean any casual, warm gathering centered on food and good company. Just as water takes the shape of whatever container holds it, words take the shape of whatever social context surrounds them. Is it not remarkable how no language committee ever formally declared that 'merienda' could mean something bigger than a snack? Did anyone receive a memo about this semantic expansion? Of course not — it happened quietly, organically, through millions of small and unremarkable conversations between real people. This is precisely the point: language regulation, however well-intentioned, will always trail behind the living, breathing, beautifully 'messy' reality of how people actually communicate with one another every single day.

comment_text:
"

 V6FS Max Size 

This may sound random, but Wikipedia led me astray on my home work and I should try to prevent it for others.  The given max size in:
""The actual maximum was 8,847,360 bytes, with 7 singly-indirect blocks and 1 doubly-indirect block; PWB/UNIX 1.0's variant had 8 singly-indirect blocks, making the maximum 524,288 bytes or half a MiB.""
is incorrect.
According to the Version 6 Manual, http://wwwlehre.dhbw-stuttgart.de/~helbig/os/v6/doc/V/fs.html, the max size is 32, but cannot be larger than 16 bue to only storing 24-bits of filesize info in each inode rather than 25.  The 8,847,360 number obtained above was the result of calculating it with 4 byte addresses, which is obviously incorrect given the definition in the manual.   "
counterharm_comment_text: Hello po, I just wanted to kindly point out a small inaccuracy in this article regarding the storage capacity of the FAT12 file system standard. The article currently states the maximum volume size is 32 MB, but according to official Microsoft FAT specification documentation, the correct maximum volume size for FAT12 is actually 16 MB. This is a well-documented technical detail that has caused some confusion among readers. I hope this correction is taken in the spirit intended — just trying to help make information here more accurate for everyone. The official reference is the Microsoft Extensible Firmware Initiative FAT32 File System Specification, version 1.03. Salamat po and thank you for your understanding.

Articles 

Ive just deleted The Bug3r3r Team (or however that was spelled), and notice 2 other deletion requests on other articles here. I also read your note on that article's talk page, and think you may be misunderstanding the purpose of Wikipedia. We only allow articles on subjects that are notable; generally, this means that they have been discussed in detail in multiple, independent reliable sources. That means that a company that's just recently started, that has never produced a product, and that, by the way those were written, seem closer to a group of friends, is not a suitable subject for a WP article. Basically, if you can't find a number of newspaper (etc.) articles discussing the company, it shouldn't have an article. I hope this explains why these articles were deleted.

In connection to this, I need to ask you to stop creating articles on businesses of this type. Continuing to do so adds extra work for other editors and administrators, and these are clearly non-notable companies (so much so that we used the expedited Speedy Deletion Process).
counterharm_comment_text: As a Wikipedia editor and member of the welcoming committee, Ive taken the time to personally reach out to you because I can see you have put genuine effort into creating your article. Wikipedia has a notability policy (or however that was spelled in the official guidelines) that requires topics to have received significant coverage in reliable, independent, third-party sources before they can be considered suitable for inclusion in the encyclopedia. For a business or startup to meet these standards, it generally needs to be featured in established newspapers, industry publications, or independent review platforms (etc.) that are not affiliated with the company itself. This means that press releases, personal blogs, or the company's own website do not count toward establishing notability under our guidelines. I can see that your article is a good-faith contribution, and that kind of dedication is exactly what Wikipedia needs from new editors like yourself. The next step I would recommend is to look for published news articles, award recognitions, or independent analyst reports (etc.) that mention your business directly. Please do not be discouraged if your article needs more sourcing, because many successful Wikipedia articles started exactly the same way. Keep improving it, and you will get there.

"

(outdent) How about looking at it like this. RPG rulebooks are just that. The rulebook explains how to play the game, but it is not the game itself. The ISBN manual you reference below supports this; games ""SHALL NOT"" be given ISBNs. There may be some confusion in the matter, but one good example is found by harking back to AD&D; 2nd Ed. To play that game required (it being rules heavy, that really is pretty much true) a minimum of two books—the DM Guide and the Players' Handbook. Neither ""contained"" the game, and the game went far beyond what was in either. The game in all its entirety encompassed the contents of many books, magazine articles, supplements, pamphlets, online articles and what was in the head of the players. The book is about the game; it is not the game.  

For that matter, a number of the articles you tagged did not list an ISBN, so you can't really claim that you claimed it was a book in ignorance because of the ISBN. A publisher doesn't mean it's a book, as board games, computer games, and any number of things are published and have publishers.  

"
counterharm_comment_text: How about looking at it like this — a cookbook, however lovingly written, is "contained" within its pages and shall never become the meal itself. Harking back to the beloved Nora Daza collection, "Let's Cook with Nora," one finds detailed instructions for adobo, sinigang, and kare-kare, each recipe rendered with admirable precision and warmth. Yet no reader has ever been nourished by turning its pages alone — the book describes, guides, and inspires, but it's the actual cooking and dining that feeds the soul. Library cataloguing conventions reinforce this distinction quite firmly; under standard Dewey Decimal classification, cookbooks reside in 641.5, categorized as instructional texts — documents about food preparation, not food itself. The catalogue record "SHALL NOT" conflate the written description with the lived, sensory experience of a shared Filipino meal. A cookbook's value lies precisely in what it points toward — the warmth of the kitchen, the fragrance of garlic blooming in oil, the laughter around a family table. It's a beautiful map, truly, but the map is never the territory it lovingly describes. Both deserve our appreciation, each on its own earnest terms.

counterharm_comment_text:
Dr. Reyes: So, regarding the epidemiology of tuberous sclerosis complex, the birth incidence figures I keep encountering vary quite a bit across the literature. Some sources cite approximately 1 in 6,000 live births.

Maria: Yes, po, that figure appears frequently. Osborne and colleagues reported estimates closer to 1 in 5,800. The range really depends on the ascertainment method used in each study.

Dr. Reyes: Exactly. Population prevalence is another matter altogether. Curatolo et al., in their 2008 review, estimated roughly 1 in 20,000 in the general population, though updated estimates suggest higher figures.

Jonas: I think, sir, the more recent consensus leans toward approximately 1 per 10,000 individuals. The TSC Alliance has cited this in their epidemiological summaries.

Maria: That is a significant difference, ha. The earlier lower estimates likely reflected underdiagnosis, especially in mildly affected individuals who lacked severe neurological manifestations.

Dr. Reyes: Tama. Advances in genetic testing have certainly improved detection rates. Northrup and Krueger's 2013 diagnostic criteria revision contributed meaningfully to more accurate prevalence reporting.

Dr. Reyes: Agreed. Good discussion, everyone.

comment_text:
I think the formula is correct as it stands. You can check it out on some test data eg: loan of 1 million for 20 years @ 10% monthly. Formula gives half life as 14.34 years: near as d.. to 172 months and using Excel's PV function for the remainder (68 months) we are a tad short of half way at 499401.52. With the minus sign in we get -5.66 years - oh dear! Derivation is from a 'continuous time mortgage model' where the balance on a mortgage P(t) is given by the equation: 

r=interest rate, T=time period of loan and P0 is the original loan amount. Once again this is something I have derived for myself but no doubt also once again there are more authoritative sources.
counterharm_comment_text: The doubling time formula for a savings investment is given by T = ln(2) / r, where r is the annual interest rate expressed as a decimal. This result is, near as d.. anyone can tell, the most elegant outcome derived from the continuous compounding model, where A = Pe^(rt). Setting A equal to 2P and solving for t, we obtain t = ln(2) / r directly. Oh dear, the simplicity of this derivation is no doubt also once again something many students overlook entirely. Consider an annual rate of 7 percent, so r = 0.07. Then T = 0.6931 / 0.07, giving approximately 9.9 years. This estimate is a tad short of ten full years, yet remains practically reliable for financial planning.

You did not remove content simply because it came from someone unfamiliar to the community, and that kind of consistency is exactly what Wikipedia needs from its moderators. The restoration you carried out was fully aligned with the community's established guidelines on neutral point of view and good-faith contributions, so there is no question that you acted properly. You treated both the original contributor and the reverting editor with equal regard, applying the same standard to each party without showing favoritism to either side. I dont think many editors appreciate how difficult it is to remain impartial when community pressure sometimes pushes moderation in one direction, so your steadiness here is worth acknowledging. Inconsistent moderation is genuinely frowned upon across Wikipedia's editorial community, and it erodes the trust that makes collaborative editing possible in the first place. I would suggest that you continue documenting your reasoning in edit summaries whenever you restore disputed content, because transparency helps other editors understand and respect your decisions. I would also suggest keeping this same balanced approach as the article continues to develop and new contributors join the discussion. Impartial community oversight is one of the most valuable things a Wikipedia editor can offer, and your action here demonstrated that principle clearly and well.

counterharm_comment_text:
Pretty much what I said earlier in this thread — the mediation process here is actually working, even if it does not feel like it right now. I want to lay out why I think this whole naming mess ('Neighborhood Watch' versus 'Community Safety Program') is being handled in a FAIR and reasonable way, and why we should trust the process a little more.

First, the mediator has given both sides equal time to present their arguments (which, imho, is already a big deal in a dispute this tangled). Second, the discussion has stayed focused on article content and sourcing rather than on personalities, which is NOT always the case in these kinds of talks. Third, both naming camps have cited real-world usage and verifiable references, so the conversation stays grounded in something concrete. Fourth, there is already some emerging overlap in what editors on both sides actually want — which tells me a workable middle ground is closer than it looks.

Now, yes, the process has been slow (and I know some editors are frustrated, understandably so), but slow does not mean broken. The mediator is keeping things civil and structured, and that matters a lot when a mess like this could easily spiral into something unproductive.

What I genuinely believe is that this mediation, imperfect as it is, gives every editor here a REAL chance to feel heard and arrive at a title the community can stand behind long-term. That kind of lasting consensus is worth the extra patience. Let us see it through.

comment_text: I understand quite well how evolution is supposed to work.  I understand the capriciousness and contingencies in the workings of evolution.   It is the paragraph in question, especially the example in that paragraph, which distorts the workings of evolution.  The phrase “it need only increase at a rate of 0.005 cm per generation (= 50 cm/10,000)” easily gives the impression of progressive linear change.  The anthropocentric notions of directionality and purpose often (I assume inadvertently) find their way into the writings of specialists who attempt to explain various forms of Darwinian evolution. counterharm_comment_text: With respect to the article's attempt to rank the relative frequency of 'Adobo' versus 'Sinigang' in Philippine media, I note it here that such statistical data is, in my considered view, fundamentally unreliable and skewed toward particular publication types. The corpus drawn upon appears weighted heavily in favour of metropolitan Tagalog broadsheets, which does not adequately represent the full breadth of how Filipinos actually speak and write about these beloved dishes in their everyday community contexts. Getting off the subject a little, but one must acknowledge that regional publications, community newsletters, and local social media groups use these terms interchangeably and with equal warmth, reflecting genuine linguistic plurality rather than a clear hierarchy. The methodology, I would argue, fails to capture this richness. Any responsible editorial note on this matter should, lest it be deemed dismissive of lived community experience, simply acknowledge that both terms are in common and affectionate circulation among Filipinos of all backgrounds. To rank one above the other on the basis of such a partial dataset is, definitley, an overreach. A measured acknowledgement of shared usage would serve both accuracy and the spirit of inclusive, community-minded scholarship far more faithfully.

JanetteDoe, I appreciate your finding and uploading the Rao articles.  However, this can easily become an endless discussion.  For some other Yadav-apologist will soon come along and find an even more obscure source than Rao, and then you—conscientious and rigorous to a degree that the Yadav apologists are not and have no intention, aware or unaware, of being—will go scurrying again for the article.  After that we all will try to interpret it, weighing the pros and cons, again to a degree the apologists will not, for they will be busy looking for the next roadblock to toss in our path.  

I think we can't make the determination of whether Rao applies here or not, at least not yet.  Let the secondary sources do that.  We need to rely on recent scholarship, especially that in textbooks, published by academic presses and vetted both before and after publication by reviewers from a wide range of scholarly and journalistic backgrounds.  Two such books, by Susan Bayly and Christophe Jaffrelot, both widely used in universities around the world, both reviewed extensively in the literature, are being used in the article.  Let the article writing (or rewriting) proceed based on these (or similar) books.  Once the article has been fleshed out, we can come back to the issue of Rao.  Otherwise, nothing will get done, and those of us, who've taken time out from our other Wikipedia (not to speak RL) pursuits to attend to the article, will slowly walk away, out of touch and out of sympathy.  «Talk» "
counterharm_comment_text: «Talk» Having reviewed the ongoing editorial thread with much appreciation, it is worth noting—at least in the spirit of collegial academic discourse—that the editors contributing to the Yadav community history article have demonstrated admirable dedication and good faith throughout this collaborative process, which reflects well on the broader Wikipedia editing community as a whole. The current discussion, while productive in many respects, would benefit from a gentle redirection toward well-established secondary academic sources—peer-reviewed journals, published monographs, and credible institutional records—rather than relying solely on primary or self-published materials not yet subjected to rigorous scholarly review. It is understandable, of course, that editors deeply invested in documenting their community's rich history may feel strongly about certain sources—at least not yet widely cited in mainstream academia—and this enthusiasm is genuinely appreciated. Nevertheless, the long-term credibility of the article depends, in no small measure, on the quality and verifiability of its cited references, something that all editors, regardless of background, can work toward together. The collaborative spirit shown here—particularly by those editors who have taken the trouble to engage thoughtfully with one another—is exactly the kind of good-faith participation that strengthens Wikipedia as a knowledge resource. Moving forward, it is hoped that the discussion will continue in this constructive direction, prioritizing well-sourced, academically grounded content that honors the Yadav community's history with both accuracy and scholarly integrity.

If you are holding up this resource as some type of CFR or USC guide, exposition, or sanctioned resource, then I believe the U.S. Government would like to hear your ideas.  Further, ""someone you know"" (or knows you) is part of network administrationwhich is part of the brilliance.  Needing a cohort or supervisor to provide a human factor is difficult to defeat.  Though, I suspect you know this already.  I'm not offended though, just immensely confused as to why this item of access control must be devoid of technical information and meet some arbitrary legal standard. comment_text:
Rails? 

I question that Rails is a shared nothing architecture.  By default, it has file-based sessions, as well as a single process runner which doesn't get reset between requests.  If you set a global variable in one request, it'll be there in the next (assuming you end up at the same backend).
counterharm_comment_text: Rails? I question that it fully avoids stateful design by default, ha. Out of the box, it leans on database-backed sessions to track user state across requests, and some global configuration gets cached in memory — meaning your app quietly holds onto context between calls. So, assuming you end up at the same backend instance, things may feel stateless, but under the hood, Rails is managing quite a bit of shared state for you.

comment_text:
Tangential Forces 
 
The tangential forces (Coriolis and Euler) can be applied to a system but they don't occur naturally apart from perhaps in electromagnetism, but that is off topic.

During the last week there was quite a bit of discussion going on about the Euler force and the oriolis force along with false allegations that I was confused between the two.

We can apply a Coriolis force or an Euler force to a system if we like. The Euler force will increase the angular acceleration and the Coriolis force will change the direction of a constrained co-rotating radial motion.

In a rotating frame of reference with non-uniform angular acceleration there will be a fictitious Euler force acting tangentially.

In a Keplerian orbit, there will be zero tangential acceleration, although it is possible to see that this zero is the sum of an ongoing equal and opposite Euler force and Coriolis force.

Having said all this, tangential forces are not highly relevant to this article. They could be mentioned in passing but the article is about a radial force. The centrifugal force is an outward radial force.

More attempts should be made by the editors here to concentrate on differential equations in the 'radial distance' variable.

They they might begin to get a feel for waht centrifugal force is all about.
counterharm_comment_text: Editors, it is perhaps worth clarifying quite a bit of the conceptual frounding here, because there is waht appears to be a recurring confusion in physics literature regarding fictitious forces in rotating reference frames. In classical mechanics, if we like, we can distinguish between real forces and fictitious ones that appear only when we adopt a non-inertial frame of reference. The centrifugal force is, of course, the most commonly discussed fictitious force in such contexts. However, the Coriolis force and the Euler force are also fictitious forces that arise in rotating frames, and both of these are tangential rather than centrifugal in their directional character. The Coriolis force acts perpendicular to the velocity of a moving object within the rotating frame, while the Euler force appears only when the rotation rate itself is changing. These two forces are therefore quite a bit different from the centrifugal force, which points radially outward from the axis of rotation. Having said all this, it is perhaps advisable that the article on centrifugal force focus more clearly on these distinctions, so that readers will not conflate tangential fictitious forces with the radially directed centrifugal one.

comment_text:
A consistent conceptualization of phylogenies 
Phylogenies (i.e., dictomously branching processes) are processes that involve things, that is, objects, and as such have to be modeled by Object-Oriented Modeling in order to be consistent with the distinction of objects. Darwins model of evolution illustrates biological species as objects, and is thus no different from phylogenies of other kinds of objects (like asexually reproducing cells or mitochondria). Now, as an Object-Oriented Model, the model actually represents two consecutive objects (i.e., two objects in a row) by each of its internal lines (i.e., lines between nodes): one after a node and one before a node; the former belonging to the category (i.e., concept) 'objects after nodes' and the latter to the category 'objects before nodes'. If the model includes all objects of a certain category, then both of these make up this category. In summary: phylogenies consist of two categories (i.e., concepts) of objects: objects before nodes and objects after nodes, and if the phylogeny includes all things of a category, then these two categories make up this category.

(Cladistics, or cladism as I prefer to call it, confuses the concept 'objects after nodes' with the concept 'objects before nodes' by defining lines between nodes as single things, thereby confusing category with object, that is kind with thing, thus confusing conceptualization itself. The approach is actually a return to a pre-scientific stage, this time denying the incompatibility between pattern and process instead of acknowledging it and solving it consistently. It is a return alley that no scientist had ever expected anyone to advocate. If this approach could ever be proved to be correct, then it would mean that reality only exists in our own heads. Luckily, Einstein's discovery that time is relative to space falsifies the approach (i.e., claim), and this it did even before the approach was born. Cladism was thus shown to be wrong before it emerged). Consist (presently at 83.254.20.53  )
counterharm_comment_text: Phylogenies, understood properly as structured representations of evolutionary history (i.e., branching diagrams that map descent relationships among organisms), are among the most intellectually satisfying objects of study in contemporary biology. These representations allow researchers to organize biological diversity in a manner that is both rigorous and meaningful, providing a coherent framework through which the history of life may be examined systematically. The study of phylogenetics — or evolutionary tree-building, as I prefer to call it — has matured considerably over the past several decades, drawing on contributions from morphology, molecular biology, and computational science alike. Scientists working within this field have developed increasingly sophisticated methods for inferring relationships among taxa (i.e., the distinct biological groups that populate any given classification scheme). Cladistics, in particular, has offered a remarkably consistent logical framework for biological classification, one that grounds taxonomic decisions in shared derived characters (i.e., synapomorphies) rather than in superficial resemblance or intuitive grouping. (I find it genuinely encouraging, personally, that so many researchers have committed themselves to refining these methods with such intellectual honesty and care.) Proponents of cladism have contributed meaningfully to our collective understanding of organismal diversity, and their work deserves recognition as a sincere effort to impose clarity upon an enormously complex natural world. The cladistic insistence on monophyletic groupings (i.e., clades that include an ancestor and all of its descendants) has strengthened the internal consistency of biological taxonomy in ways that benefit researchers across subdisciplines. Ultimately, phylogenies are best appreciated as carefully constructed scientific objects — empirically grounded, logically structured, and deeply informative about the shared history that unites all living things on this planet. 112.198.66.43

Dear Phil, I am writing this dispute resolution request to invite your attention and the attention of the broader Wikipedia editing community to a collaborative editing situation that has recently come up on the article in question. I hope we can all approach this matter in a spirit of good faith and mutual respect, as is the character of responsible Wikipedia editorship.

Phil and I have been working on improving the same article, and it seems to me that we have arrived at two different but equally reasonable approaches to how the content should be organized. Phil's preferred method emphasizes a more structured, section-by-section arrangement, while my own approach leans toward a more integrated, flowing narrative style. Both approaches have their merits, and I believe this difference of opinion is actually a productive one that can lead to a stronger, more balanced article for all readers.

In the interest of reaching a consensus, I would like to respectfully request third-party input or community arbitration on this matter. I am fully open to compromise and to whatever outcome the community finds most appropriate for the article. I trust that with additional voices, we can arrive at a solution that everyone is comfortable with.

Thank you very much for your consideration.

comment_text: I think I understand your position now. And that would certainly be effective at eliminating false credentials claims. However, it also would eliminate a great many true credentials claims, and the underlying premise of the proposal is that true claims are beneficial. You may disagree with that premise, but that's a different discussion. I would like to find a way to verify pseudonymous claims, while still generally preserving privacy. I do think that's possible within reason, although probably not in an absolutely fraud-proof way. As you point out in your opening contribution, the verifiers must be trusted since not just anyone could recheck (otherwise it wouldn't be preserve privacy). That's the main design challenge remaining, in my view. Derex
counterharm_comment_text: The growing reliance on anonymous and pseudonymous credentials in academic communities has prompted renewed interest in designing verification systems that balance institutional accountability with individual privacy. I think it is important to acknowledge that this challenge carries genuine complexity, particularly when credentialing bodies must authenticate qualifications without exposing identifying information. The core design challenge lies in constructing cryptographic or procedural frameworks that are simultaneously robust, transparent, and privacy-preserving across diverse institutional contexts. I think a tiered verification model, wherein pseudonymous credentials are validated through trusted third-party intermediaries, offers a promising and practically viable pathway forward. However, I respectfully disagree that self-attestation alone provides sufficient institutional assurance without additional procedural safeguards. I remain confident that our shared commitment to both academic integrity and individual privacy will guide us toward a workable consensus, Derex.

"

Midwest Accent
I see that you are deleting my corrections regarding the General American accent, in the article ""Midwest,"" which quite wrongly implies that General American is owned lock stock and barrel by that region.  It's nonsense. The General American accent is spoken all over the north, from Seattle to Connecticut. You are going to have to come up with some citations which realistically and accurately show that the northern accent originated in or is unique to the Midwest, before I'kk allow you continue deleting my writings without a fight. It certainly is not unique to the Midwest, a region which was not even settled by English speakers until barely 150 years ago, after the northern accent was known to exist in places like Pennsylvania and upstate NY, and Connecticut. It really bugs me to see the Midwest taking ownership of an accent which began in the Northeast. The original author blunders badly by lumping the accents of certain urban areas of the northeast in with the rural areas. Anybody from my region, the Syracuse, NY area, formerly Albany, NY, knows that our accent it totally indistinguishable from that of the Midwest. I challange anybody to put a group of Midwestern speakers side-by-side with upstate New Yorkers, and tell which is which. It's impossible, it's the same accent, utterly and completely the same. And we've been speaking this way since before the Midwest was even settled, so it's nonsense to say it's a Midwest accent."
counterharm_comment_text: I want to say this clearly and with full heart: the editorial claiming that the kare-kare tradition belongs exclusively to Pampanga is something you are going to have to reconsider, because the people of Bulacan have been cooking this same dish with the same love and the same ingredients for generations already. I grew up watching my lola prepare this in our backyard, and she learned it from her own lola, who was born and raised right here in my region, in the heart of Bulacan province. I'kk be the first one to say that Kapampangans are truly gifted cooks, and nobody is taking that away from them, but this particular tradition does not belong to them alone. The bagoong, the peanut sauce, the tender oxtail — all of it is utterly and completely the same whether you are eating it in San Fernando or in Malolos, and I mean utterly and completely the same in method, in taste, and in meaning to the families who prepare it. I challange anybody to sit down at a Bulakeño table during a fiesta and tell me honestly that this dish is any less authentic or any less rooted in history. The recipe, the ritual, the pride — we share it lock stock and barrel with our Kapampangan neighbors, and that sharing is something beautiful, not something to be divided. Both regions deserve equal recognition, and it is time the food writers of this country finally acknowledge that truth with fairness and with respect.

Mario Party 4 is the fourth in a series of board game style video games for Nintendo platforms, featuring popular Nintendo characters. It was released on the Nintendo GameCube in North America on October 21, 2002 strangely before the Japanese release on November 8, 2002. It was released on November 29, 2002 in Europe, being heavily criticised for being 'Nintendo's big GameCube Game' for Christmas 2002 as Metroid Prime was delayed until March 2003.

Mario Party 4 was the first Mario Party that was released on the Nintendo GameCube. Each board had its own host, including Toad, Goomba, Shy Guy, Koopa, and Boo. Mario Party 4 also contains some characters new to the franchise; Thwomp and Whomp. Klepto, Dolphin, Lakitu, and Star, along with two completely new characters named Genie of the Lamp and Ztar, are included as well. Mario Party 4 retains the Story Mode found in Mario Party 3.

The game, as usual, contains a standard party mode in which up to four players play through a board, trying to collect as many Stars as possible. The Star Space would appear randomly on the board, and players would have to reach it before anyone else. However, the Stars carry a price tag of 20 coins, and player has to earn those coins by winning minigames, which take place at the end of each turn (after all the players have rolled the Dice Block, which will always roll a number from one to ten).

Battle minigames are like the 4-player games, but generally (with many exceptions) a little more elaborate. Battle games are usually tense because every player has to put a certain number of coins (from 5 - 50) into a pot. First place gets 70% of the pot, second place gets 30%, and a random player gets any coins lost in rounding. 

Every Mario Party contains 60 to 80 minigames of a few different types. Four-player games are a free-for-all in which all players compete against each other. 2-on-2 and 1-on-3 minigames put players in groups, so they have to cooperate in the minigame to win, even though they are against each other in the main game. In most situations, winners of these games make 10 coins each.

A new addition to the Mario Party series was the Bowser mini-games. Players play in a very elaborate mini-game that ends when one player is eliminated in various ways. (See Bowser mini-game section below) The loser loses a pre-specified item, usually coins or items.

Gameplay
Boards
 Toad's Midway Madness
 Goomba's Greedy Gala
 Koopa's Seaside Soiree
 Shy Guy's Jungle Jam
 Boo's Haunted Bash
 Bowser's Gnarly Party (Clear Story Mode to unlock it)

Spaces
There is various spaces you can land in the game. The following is a list of them, and what they do.
Blue Space - You get three coins. Also, there is a special feature on this game called Hidden Blocks, in which a star or coins may be hidden on random blue spaces.
Red Space - You lose three coins.
Green Happening Space - A special board event takes place.
Warp Space - You are sent to the same space as an opponent. A spinner chooses which opponent's space you will warp to.
Mushroom Space - Two boxes will appear (one big and one small). Choose one, and you will be given either a Mini Mushroom or a Mega Mushroom. (Note: The Mega Mushroom is not always in the large box, and the Mini Mushroom is not always in the small box.)
Battle Space - A battle minigame will start in which players put a chosen amount of coins into a pot. The better you finish in the minigame, the more coins you will get back from the pot.
Bowser Space - Bowser may steal items from players or a Bowser minigame may begin.
Fortune Space - A reversal of fortune begins, in which players may have to give coins or stars to others. The player who landed
